title: Automatic error bounds for real roots of polynomials having interval coefficients
abstract: A generalised Newton-Raphson method for use with interval arithmetic is described. This algorithm can, for example, be used with great effectiveness to obtain precise bounds for the real roots of real polynomials whose coefficients are not exactly known. Two eigenvalue problems for real matrices are given as examples.

title: Conditionally convergent spectral expansions
abstract: <jats:p>We will consider a reflexive Banach space 𝔅, with real or complex scalars, and a bounded operator in 𝔅 with a real spectrum.</jats:p>

title: Problems in real and complex analysis
abstract:
This book consists of two sections. The first section ``Real analysis'' is a revised and enlarged version of the earlier book Problems in analysis.


The second section ``Complex analysis'' contains problems distributed among many of the principal topics in the theory of functions of a complex variable.


The book contains 878 problems and their solutions. The problems in the book cover, in real analysis: set algebra, measure and topology, real- and complex-valued functions, topological vector spaces; and in complex analysis: polynomials and power series, functions holomorphic in a region, entire functions, analytic continuation, singularities, harmonic functions, families of functions, convexity theorems. The aim of the problems is to sharpen and deepen the understanding of the mechanisms that underlie modern analysis. So classroom instructors, students and other interested mathematicians can use this problem collection for practice.
venue: Problem Books in Mathematics. New York: Springer-Verlag. ix, 488 p. (1992).

title: Real analysis. A comprehensive course in analysis, part 1
abstract:
The volume under review presents a clear, thorough treatment of the theorems and concepts of real analysis. The author is concerned with the fundamentals and touchstone results of the real analysis in full rigor, but in a style that requires little prior familiarity with proofs or mathematical language.

The book develops those concepts and tools in real analysis that are vital to every mathematician, whether pure or applied, aspiring or established. These works present a comprehensive treatment with a global view of the subject, emphasizing the connections between real analysis and other branches of mathematics.

From a first point of view, the volume presents the infinitesimal calculus of the twentieth century with the ultimate integral calculus (measure theory) and the ultimate differential calculus (distribution theory). From another point of view, it shows the triumph of abstract spaces such as topological spaces, Banach and Hilbert spaces, measure spaces, Riesz spaces, Polish spaces, locally convex spaces, Fréchet spaces, Schwartz space, and Lebesgue \(L^p\)-spaces.

The text includes many elegant proofs and an excellent choice of topics, including the Fourier series and transform, dual spaces, the Baire category, fixed point theorems, probability ideas, and Hausdorff dimension. The book includes plenty of relevant applications, for instance the constructions of nowhere differentiable functions, Brownian motion, space-filling curves, solutions of the moment problem, Haar measure, and equilibrium measures in potential theory. Numerous examples and exercises are integrated into the main text as well as the end of each chapter to reinforce the methodology.

With clear proofs, detailed examples, and numerous exercises, this book gives a thorough treatment of the subject. It provides a logical development of material that will prepare readers for more advanced analysis-based studies. This volume prepares the reader for further exploration of measure theory, functional analysis, harmonic analysis, and beyond. The clarity and breadth of \textit{Real analysis. A comprehensive course in analysis, part 1} make this volume a welcome addition to the personal library of every mathematician.
venue: Providence, RI: American Mathematical Society (AMS) (ISBN 978-1-4704-1099-5/hbk). xx, 789~p. (2015).

title: A first course in real analysis
abstract:
The specific of the author's approach consists of a clever combination of what is usually called calculus and of what usually belongs to real analysis. This ``first course in real analysis'' could also be interpreted as a second reading of calculus. Main attention is given to ideas, concepts, theorems, types of reasoning and less to routine aspects and computation technics. This explains how the author succeeds to avoid the obesity we see at most textbooks of mathematical analysis and to perform the itinerary from real numbers to Lebesgue integral on only 230 pages.


The context: Axioms for the field \(\mathbb{R}\) of real numbers; First properties of \(\mathbb{R}\); Sequences of real numbers; Convergence; Special subsets of \(\mathbb{R}\); Continuity; Continuous functions on an interval; Limits of functions; Derivatives; Riemann integral; Infinite series; Beyond the Riemann integral. Appendix. Index of notations.


It's a pity, there is no final section devoted to the Kurzweil-Henstock integral in order to make possible to integrate any finite derivative.
venue: Undergraduate Texts in Mathematics. New York: Springer-Verlag. xi, 237 p. (1994).

title: Holomorphic extension of analytic functionals defined on a real Wiener space, Cauchy formula, stationary phase
abstract: Summary: Real analytic functionals on a real Wiener space \(X\) for the Sobolev norms; complexification \(X^C\) of \(X\); holomorphic extension to \(X^C\) of a real analytic functional; integration on totally real submanifolds of \(X^C\) of ``codimension \(\infty/2\)''; Cauchy integral formula; holomorphic prolongation of the stochastic integral of a real analytic process; method of the stationary phase in a real analytic framework.
venue: C. R. Acad. Sci., Paris, Sér. I 322, No. 3, 261-265 (1996).

title: Fundamental ideas of analysis
abstract: This book is intended for use as a textbook in a first undergraduate course in real analysis. It begins much as usual with the ordered field properties of real numbers, after which the first chapter closes with a section on methods of proof, in which such matters as the distinction between \(P\) implies \(Q\) and \(Q\) implies \(P\), direct proof versus contrapositive proof and proof by contradiction, proof by induction and the like are discussed. In Chapter 2 we have convergence of sequences, sup and inf, the completeness of the reals and the Bolzano-Weierstrass theorem in one dimension, among other topics. The Riemann integral is introduced in Chapter 3, and in Chapter 4 we meet differentiation and the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus. Chapters 5 and 6 on sequences and series of functions round out the material suggested for a one-semester course, if the optional sections are omitted. In the optional sections, two-state Makov chains, sequences defined recursively via a quadratic map, numerical methods, Newton's method, a section on differentiation of functions of two variables, calculus of variations, normed linear spaces, complex numbers and infinite products and prime numbers are treated. The remaining four chapters, on differential equations, complex analysis, Fourier series, and probability theory allow the expansion of the book's use to a two-semester course which makes connections to several branches of mathematics and other sciences in which real analysis has a significant role to play, and which are not always included in a course in real analysis. There are plenty of good exercises provided with each section. The book should be an excellent candidate for the basic text in a first course in real analysis for undergraduates, not only for mathematics majors but for students in all areas where real analysis plays a role.

title: Real spectra in non-Hermitian Hamiltonians having \(\mathcal{PT}\) symmetry
abstract: Summary: The condition of self-adjointness ensures that the eigenvalues of a Hamiltonian are real and bounded below. Replacing this condition by the weaker condition \(\mathcal P\mathcal T\) of symmetry, one obtains new infinite classes of complex Hamiltonians whose spectra are also real and positive. These \(\mathcal P\mathcal T\) symmetric theories may be viewed as analytic continuations of conventional theories from real to complex phase space. This paper describes the unusual classical and quantum properties of these theories.

title: Real analysis
abstract:
This is the second edition of the book on advanced real analysis. The first edition was published by Birkhäuser in 2002 [Zbl 1012.26001] and became one of the main textbooks on this subject. In order to make the book more practical for applications, the author add a new chapter related to weak differentiability. The titles of all other chapters remain the same tough the presentation is essentially revised (especially in the last four chapters of the previous edition).


This new edition of the book by E. DiBenedetto can be characterised as follows: the self-contained textbook on modern topics of real analysis with emphasis on possible applications to partial differential equations and probability theory. The book is written for mathematicians (as for experts in analysis, as for students with a good knowledge in first-year analysis). Another side of the book is its encyclopedic character. It contains the most of known (and useful) results of analysis of XX century presented in a rigorous but intelligible form. The book can be used also for self-study. To reach this goal the author supplies the main text with a large set of examples as well as with a series of exercises (solved or proposed to be solved by the reader) and instructive additional facts on the considered topic. All these exercises and facts are gathered in subsections ``Problems and complements'' at the end of each chapter. The reader can find many interesting details which serve to illuminate the diamonds of analysis. The list of references contains the main books and articles which form the modern real analysis.


The book can be recommended as one of the main readings on real analysis for those who are interested in this subject and its numerous applications.


The titles of the chapters give the reader an idea of the content of the book: 1. Preliminaries. 2. Topologies and metric spaces. 3. Measuring sets. 4. The Lebesgue integral. 5. Topics on measurable functions of real variables. 6. The \(L^p\) spaces. 7. Banach spaces. 8. Spaces of continuous functions, distributions and weak derivatives. 9. Topics of integrable functions of real variables. 10. Embeddings of \(W^{1,p}(E)\) into \(L^q(E)\). 11. Topics on weakly differentiable functions.
venue: Birkhäuser Advanced Texts. Basler Lehrbücher. New York, NY: Birkhäuser/Springer (ISBN 978-1-4939-4003-5/hbk; 978-1-4939-4005-9/ebook). xxxii, 596~p. (2016).

title: Real and complex analysis
abstract:
There is a vast literature of textbooks on mathematical analysis, both real and complex. Walter Rudin is well-known as the author of one of the best textbooks on ``Principles of mathematical analysis'' (1953; Zbl 0052.05301, and many other editions) which now has been translated into 13 languages. The most recent edition of the book under review has appeared 3 years ago (1997) and has also found the worldwide distribution it deserves. It is really good news that now a German translation is available, too, at a reasonable price.


The book succeeds in bridging the somewhat artificial gap between real and complex analysis and covers surprisingly many important fields of advanced mathematical analysis, such as measure and integration, Hilbert and Banach spaces, Fourier transforms, harmonic functions and conformal mappings, Lebesgue and Hardy spaces, and approximation problems. A more detailed description of the contents may be found in the review of the Spanish translation (1985; Zbl 0613.26001). Without any doubt, this book may be enthusiastically recommended to every teacher and student, as well as to researchers in other fields of mathematics who have already given up looking for a good presentation of real and complex analysis as a whole.

title: What can geometric measure theory do for several complex variables!
abstract:
Complex analysis has provided both examples and inspiration to geometric measure theory. We wish to suggest ways in which part of real analysis might also be useful in complex geometry and to put forward possible new directions for interaction.


Modern geometric measure theory began in 1960 with the works of De Giorgi, Federer, Fleming and Reifenberg in which fundamental applications were discovered in the geometric calculus of variations, especially in the problem of least area. An important connection with complex analysis came with the realization that pieces of singular holomorphic varieties minimize area in competition with all oriented surfaces having the same boundary. In particular, complex varieties are extremal surfaces in a much larger real environment. There are various correspondences between complex analytic functions and varieties on the one hand and harmonic functions and minimal surfaces on the other. Because of these relations, one can sometimes use methods from real analysis to establish interesting estimates in complex geometry.

For the entire collection see [Zbl 0759.00008].
venue: Several complex variables, Proc. Mittag-Leffler Inst., Stockholm/Swed. 1987-88, Math. Notes 38, 8-21 (1993).

title: One-parameter semigroups of subsets of a real linear space
abstract:
A one-parameter semigroup of subsets of a linear space \(L\) is a \(1:1\) function \(\delta\to A(\delta)\) sending the real positive numbers into the set of all subsets of \(L\) and satisfying \(A(\delta_1) + A(\delta_2) = A(\delta_1 + \delta_2)\), where \(K+M\) is the set of all sums \(k+m\) with \(k\in K\), \(m\in M\). The following very deep result is proved: 

Any one-parameter semigroup of compact sets in a locally convex Hausdorff linear space is of the form \(A(\delta) = f(\delta) + \delta K\), where \(f(\delta)\) is a one-parameter semigroup of points, \(K\) is a compact convex set, and \(\delta K\) is multiplication by the real \(\delta\).

title: Problems in real and functional analysis
abstract: ``The heart of mathematics is its problems'', said Paul Halmos. Solving problems definitely helps students to understand mathematics well. This book provides 1457 problems in real and functional analysis at the graduate level. The book consists of 20 chapters. Chapters 1 to 10 contain problems and the remaining chapters contain selected solutions. Chapters 1 to 7 deal with some topics in real analysis such as measure theory and \(L^p\)-spaces, and Chapters 8 to 10 cover the topics normed spaces, Hilbert spaces and linear operators in functional analysis. Each chapter starts with some definitions and basic results in the area needed to understand the problems. The book is written in a very clear style and is very useful for graduate students to extend their vision of real and functional analysis.
venue: Graduate Studies in Mathematics 166. Providence, RI: American Mathematical Society (AMS) (ISBN 978-1-4704-2057-4/hbk). x, 467~p. (2015).

title: Real analysis
abstract:
The book is a clear and structured introduction to real analysis. The core is represented by the Chapters 2. Sequences and series, 3. Functions and continuity, 4. Differentiation, and 5. The Riemann integral.


Chapter 6 introduces the logarithmic and exponential functions. Sequences and series of functions, together with the crucial notion of uniform convergence, are presented in Chapter 7. Chapter 8 is devoted to the circular functions. Wallis's formula, Stirling's formula and an example of a continuous, nowhere differentiable function are presented in Chapter 9. Fully worked out examples and exercises with solutions extend and illustrate the theory.


Written in an easy-to-read style, combining informality and precision, the book is ideal for self-study or as a course textbook for first- and second-year undergraduates.
venue: Springer Undergraduate Mathematics Series. London: Springer. x, 276 p. \textsterling 15.95; \$ 29.95; sFr. 45.00; öS 365.00 (2001).

title: An introduction to analysis
abstract:
The book contains properties of the real numbers as an ordered field, sequences of real numbers, topology of the real numbers, limits and continuity, differentiation of real functions of a real variable, the Riemann integral, the Riemann-Stieltjes integral, series of real numbers, sequences and series of functions and Fourier series. The book is written very carefully. It contains also a lot of interesting exercises. 


Reviewer's remark: On the page 169 it is lack of any discussion about the independence of the Riemann-Stieltjes integral \(\int^{b}_{a}f dg\) from the representation \(g=g_ 1-g_ 2.\)
venue: Boston, MA: PWS-KENT Publishing Company. xii, 276 p. \textsterling 15.95 (1989).

title: Klassische elementare Analysis. (Classical elementary analysis)
abstract:
Das vorliegende Buch ist nicht einfach ein weiterer Beitrag zur unübersehbaren Flut von Analysisbüchern, die derzeit auf dem Markt sind. Es soll vielmehr einige zentrale Ergebnisse der reellen Analysis besonders gründlich und vielseitig beleuchten und damit, so der Wunsch des Verfassers, ``den Leser zu einer Beschäftigung mit Mathematik stimulieren''. Der Stoff ist in der Tat didaktisch ansprechend aufbereitet und trotzdem mathematisch substantiell, und kann daher gut sowohl als Ergänzung zu einem der ``üblichen'' Analysis-Textbücher als auch als Vorlage für Proseminare dienen. 


Das Buch besteht aus 6 Kapiteln. Nach dem 1. Kapitel ``Der goldene Schnitt'', welches wohl in erster Linie den Laien an die mathematische Sprache gewöhnen soll, geht es im 2. Kapitel ``Folgen und Reihen reeller Zahlen'' ``richtig los''. Besonderes Gewicht wird hier auf Näherungs- und Darstellungsformeln für die Riemannsche Zetafunktion gelegt; dieses Thema durchzieht überhaupt das ganze Buch als roter Faden. Das 3. Kapitel ``Das Riemannsche Integral und der Logarithmus'' ist der (glücklicherweise korrekten) Definition des Integrals und elementaren Integrationstechniken sowie den Eigenschaften der Exponential- und Logarithmusfunktion gewidmet. Die Diskussion über die Integration als ``Umkehrung'' der Differentiation mag als Beispiel dafür dienen, wie erfreulich präzise der Autor bei aller Allgemeinverständlichkeit den Dingen auf den Grund geht; dies ist ja bekanntlich bei Büchern, die von sogenannten ``Didaktikern'' geschrieben werden, fast nie der Fall. Kapitel 4 über ``algebraische und zahlentheoretische Anwendungen'' fällt am deutlichsten aus dem für Analysisbücher üblichen Rahmen. Hier werden Teilungsprobleme, Summationsformeln, Rationalitätsfragen und nochmals die Zetafunktion diskutiert. Schließlich ist Kapitel 5 der ``Erzeugung von Funktionen durch unendliche Reihen'' gewidmet, d. h. Funktionenfolgen und -reihen und damit verbundenen ``Vertauschungsproblemen''. 


Das letzte Kapitel mit dem vielversprechenden Namen ``Perlen der elementaren Analysis'' behandelt einige Aspekte der Eulerschen und Poissonschen Summationsformeln sowie Sätze über die Eulersche Gammafunktion (Bohr-Mollerup usw.). Dieses Kapitel ist natürlich besonders geeignet als Vorlage für ein Proseminar für Studenten etwa des 3. Semesters, sowie auch als Ergänzung zum Büchlein ``Große Sätze und schöne Beweise der Mathematik'' von \textit{J. Naas} and \textit{W. Tutschke} [Akad.-Verlag Berlin (1986)]. 


Im großen und ganzen ist es dem Autor gelungen, den Worten ``klassisch'' und ``elementar'' im Titel treu zu bleiben und trotzdem ein nichttriviales Werk zu verfassen, welches Studenten und Dozenten gleichermaßen als Ergänzung ihrer Heimbibliothek empfohlen werden kann.
venue: Basel - Boston: Birkhäuser Verlag. 211 S.; DM 44.00 (1987).

title: Real analysis and foundations
abstract:
This is the second extended and enlarged edition of the textbook published in 1991 by CRC Press [Zbl 0757.26002]. The book combines two features. First of all it serves as a textbook on the most essential parts of real analysis. Second, it introduces many of the creative ideas of real analysis. To make this combination the author tries to organize the text as simple as possible. It is achieved by choosing the most important results from different chapters of Analysis, supplying them by clear and straightforward proofs, and by illustrating of these results by series of very simple but instructive examples and exercises.

The book consists of several parts: Introduction, presented by Logic and set theory, as well as number theory; Concept of limit, described in the chapters Sequences, Series of numbers, Basic topology, Limits and continuity of functions; differentiation and integration in one and several variables, including elementary transcendental functions and applications to the study of basic types of ordinary differential equations; modern analysis, presented by essential description of harmonic analysis, and Introduction to wavelet analysis; basic notion of analysis containing an instructive discussion of metric spaces and topology, Lebesgue integration and Kolmogorov's axiomatic of probability theory.

The book is recommended as a source for middle-level mathematical courses. It can be used not only at mathematical department, but also by physicists, engineers, economists and other experts in applied sciences who want to understand the main ideas of analysis in order to use them at the study of mathematical models of different type processes.
venue: Studies in Advanced Mathematics. Boca Raton, FL: Chapman \& Hall/CRC (ISBN 1-58488-483-5/hbk). xvi, 454~p. (2005).

title: Probabilistic representation of solutions for quasi-linear parabolic PDE via FBSDE with reflecting boundary conditions
abstract: Summary: A probabilistic representation of the solution (in the viscosity sense) of a quasi-linear parabolic PDE system with non-Lipschitz terms and Neumann boundary conditions is given via a fully coupled forward-backward stochastic differential equation with a reflecting term in the forward equation. The extension of previous results consists of the relaxation of the Lipschitz assumption on the drift coefficient of the forward equation, using a previous result of the authors.
venue: Bol. Soc. Esp. Mat. Apl., S\(\vec{\text{e}}\)MA 51, 109-116 (2010).

title: Vector valued Orlicz spaces, II
abstract: In this paper properties of linear spaces generated by GNfunctions, which are called vector valued Orlicz spaces, are studied. The class of G-functions were introduced and studied by the author in the paper Vector Valued Orlicz Spaces, I. This work extends the usual theory of Orlicz spaces generated by real valued -functions of a real variable. In particular, GN-functions are a generalization of the variable N-functions used by Portnov and the nondecreasing N-functions by Wang.

title: Genetic algorithms and Walsh functions. II: Deception and its analysis
abstract:
The paper develops a number of tools for analyzing and designing the interaction of codings, functions and operators within genetic algorithms. Whereas Part I [see the above review] has considered the application of Walsh functions to the analysis of the above mentioned algorithms, this paper extends those methods to permit a rigorous analysis of deception.


The concepts of static deception and simplicity are defined in terms of the overlap of two different near-optimal subsets: one is defined in terms of fitness and the other, in terms of operator-adjusted fitness.


The author derives procedures for analyzing deception using algebraic methods. A sensitivity analysis is proposed for constrained function analysis when penalty methods are imposed. A sensitivity analysis procedure has been outlined to permit the direct evaluation of the change in deception that can occur when one or more of the function's value change.


A number of extensions of the presented methods are discussed, including analysis of different operators, coding and non binary alphabets.

title: Harmonic analysis on symmetric spaces as complex analysis
abstract:
Based on several decades of studying complex analysis on symmetric spaces, the author expresses his belief that complex analysis may play an important role in harmonic analysis on real symmetric spaces as well. First, he considers the horospherical reduction of complex symmetric spaces and discusses the dual horospherical Cauchy transform, also presenting a Cauchy formula on complex symmetric spaces. Next, he presents the horospherical duality for real symmetric spaces and for complex crowns of symmetric spaces. Finally, he discusses tube Stein manifolds on causal symmetric spaces. His historical and heuristic remarks concerning the subject make this paper very interesting.

For the entire collection see [Zbl 1286.11002].
venue: Cogdell, James W. (ed.) et al., Automorphic forms and related geometry: assessing the legacy of I. I. Piatetski-Shapiro. Proceedings of the conference, Yale University, New Haven, CT, USA, April 23--27, 2012. Providence, RI: American Mathematical Society (AMS) (ISBN 978-0-8218-9394-4/pbk). Contemporary Mathematics 614, 69-80 (2014).

title: Overview of object oriented data analysis
abstract: Summary: Object oriented data analysis is the statistical analysis of populations of complex objects. In the special case of functional data analysis, these data objects are curves, where a variety of Euclidean approaches, such as principal components analysis, have been very successful. Challenges in modern medical image analysis motivate the statistical analysis of populations of more complex data objects that are elements of mildly non-Euclidean spaces, such as lie groups and symmetric spaces, or of strongly non-Euclidean spaces, such as spaces of tree-structured data objects. These new contexts for object oriented data analysis create several potentially large new interfaces between mathematics and statistics. The notion of object oriented data analysis also impacts data analysis, through providing a framework for discussion of the many choices needed in many modern complex data analyses, especially in interdisciplinary contexts.

title: Nirenberg's contributions to complex analysis
abstract:
This article gives an overview over the fundamental contributions of Louis Nirenberg to complex analysis and their impact on the theory of partial differential equations. It explains the main results of Nirenberg and sketches the developments that they engendered. The author of the present paper is one of the real experts in the field and a main collaborator of Nirenberg. The material is put together in a nice order and gives a good overview of some main developments in complex analysis which are related to partial differential equations.

Problems in complex analysis have often been studied with the aid of the theory of partial differential equations. Conversely, important advances in the theory of partial differential equations have come from the study of problems in complex analysis. In the last half century the interactions between these fields have been very fruitful and Louis Nirenberg was a leading participant in these developments. This article describes some of Nirenberg's contributions to complex analysis and some of his related work in partial differential equations. Topics covered are the Newlander-Nirenberg theorem and the Nirenberg-Frobenius theorem on integrability, applications of these to deformations of complex structures, the \(\overline{\partial}\)-Neumann problem (and subelliptic estimates), the theory of pseudodifferential operators, degenerate elliptic-parabolic equations, holomorphic support functions, local embeddings of pseudoconvex CR-manifolds, biholomorphic invariants, the complex Monge-Ampère equation.

title: Harmonic analysis on symmetric Stein manifolds from the point of view of complex analysis
abstract: The author studies the analysis on complex semisimple Lie groups, but he also considers a more general class of objects -- symmetric Stein manifolds with complex groups of automorphisms. He uses a complex version of horospherical transform on real symmetric spaces (in a certain sense, the \(\delta\)-functions are replaced by Cauchy kernels). Thus, the classical theory of finite dimensional representations of compact and complex semisimple Lie groups is discussed from the perspective of multidimensional complex geometry and analysis. The complex horospherical transform establishes a duality between the spaces of holomorphic functions on symmetric Stein manifolds and dual horospherical manifolds.

title: A high-speed algorithm for the computer generation of Fourier transforms
abstract: Abstract—An efficient algorithm is presented for performing the Fourier transformation operation on a digital computer. This algorithm completes the transform calculation with Nlog <inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> N complex additions (complex additions include complex subtractions) and ½N [log <inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> (N)-2]+1 complex multiplications. This is a considerable savings over a brute-force method of N <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> complex additions and multiplications, especially when considering the use of digital machines whose MULTIPLY time is much longer than ADD time.

title: Outline of a new approach to the analysis of complex systems and decision processes
abstract: The approach described in this paper represents a substantive departure from the conventional quantitative techniques of system analysis. It has three main distinguishing features: 1) use of so-called ``linguistic'' variables in place of or in addition to numerical variables; 2) characterization of simple relations between variables by fuzzy conditional statements; and 3) characterization of complex relations by fuzzy algorithms. A linguistic variable is defined as a variable whose values are sentences in a natural or artificial language. Thus, if tall, not tall, very tall, very very tall, etc. are values of height, then height is a linguistic variable. Fuzzy conditional statements are expressions of the form IF A THEN B, where A and B have fuzzy meaning, e.g., IF x is small THEN y is large, where small and large are viewed as labels of fuzzy sets. A fuzzy algorithm is an ordered sequence of instructions which may contain fuzzy assignment and conditional statements, e.g., x = very small, IF x is small THEN Y is large. The execution of such instructions is governed by the compositional rule of inference and the rule of the preponderant alternative. By relying on the use of linguistic variables and fuzzy algorithms, the approach provides an approximate and yet effective means of describing the behavior of systems which are too complex or too ill-defined to admit of precise mathematical analysis.
venue: IEEE Trans. Syst. Man Cybern. 3, No. 1, 28-44 (1973).

title: The complex Bingham distribution and shape analysis
abstract: Let \({\mathfrak z}\) be a \(k\)-dimensional complex vector and let \({\mathfrak C}S^{k-1}= \{{\mathfrak z}\): \(|{\mathfrak z}|=1\}\) denote the unit complex sphere in \({\mathfrak C}^ k\). The complex Bingham distribution on \({\mathfrak C}S^{k-1}\) is defined by the probability density function \(f({\mathfrak z})= c(A)^{-1}\exp {\mathfrak z}^* A{\mathfrak z}\), \({\mathfrak z}\in {\mathfrak C}S^{k-1}\), where \({\mathfrak z}^*\) is the complex conjugate of the transpose of \({\mathfrak z}\). \(c(A)\) is a normalizing constant, and the parameter matrix \(A\) is a \(k\times k\) Hermitian matrix. Properties of this distribution are studied and statistical applications considered.

title: The extension property of complex Banach spaces
abstract: The structure of real normed linear spaces with the extension propertywas clarified by Nachbin [10], Goodner Such a space is known to be equivalent to the Banach space of all real-valued continuous functions on a suitable stonean space with the topology of uniform norm. In connection with this, it has been conjectured that an analogous theorem will hold for complex normed linear spaces (cf. Grothendieck [7]). The object of the present note is to give an affirmative solution to this problem by utilizing the device of Kelley [8]. In our discussion, a theorem on continuous selections, proved in 1, enables us to apply well the device of Kelley to our case.

title: Hidden harmony -- geometric fantasies. The rise of complex function theory
abstract:
This book is the first one devoted to the history of complex function theory. The authors present the rise of analytic function theory from its origins to 1914.

The book contains ten chapters whose titles are as follows: Elliptic functions; From real to complex analysis; Cauchy's ``modern analysis''; Complex functions and elliptic integrals; Riemann's geometric function theory; Weierstrass's analytic function theory; Complex function theory and differential equations; Advanced topics in the theory of functions; Several complex variables; The textbook tradition.

The authors describe contributions of great mathematicians starting from the founders of the subject -- Cauchy, Riemann and Weierstrass -- through d'Alembert, Poincaré, Laplace, Weyl and others. Basing on published and unpublished sources the book presents the development and role of elliptic function theory, differential equations in the complex domain, geometric function theory and the early years of complex function theory in several variables.

This book is of great interest and help, not only for mathematicians interested in complex function theory, but also for everyone who likes the history of mathematics.
venue: Sources and Studies in the History of Mathematics and Physical Sciences. New York, NY: Springer (ISBN 978-1-4614-5724-4/hbk; 978-1-4614-5725-1/ebook). xvii, 848~p. (2013).

title: Advanced complex analysis. A comprehensive course in analysis, part 2B
abstract:
Complex analysis is one of the core fields of analysis. It is not only an extremely beautiful and aesthetic branch of analysis but also one with interrelations with almost all other topics in analysis and its applications. Complex analysis is of importance for many other fields of mathematics and it is a melting pot of almost all kinds of structural concepts in mathematics: algebraic, analytic, topologic, and geometric.

This is the third volume of a five volume set of books that is on graduate analysis. This is a substantial text dealing with advanced complex analysis that can be used by students of various kinds (in particular, by both pure and applied mathematicians) and at different levels. Thus, it contains all the standard elementary material but also, on the one hand, sections devoted to topics like the Poincaré metric, Ahlfors-Robinson proof of Picard's theorem, Bergmann kernel, Painlevé's conformal mapping theorem, Jacobi theorems, Dirichlet series, Dirichlet's prime progression theorem, zeta function, prime number theorem, Bessel and Airy functions, Hankel and Sommerfeld contours, Nevanlinna's theorems.

One of the features of this volume is a thorough introduction of the theory of conformal metrics (including the Poincaré metric, the Ahlfors-Robinson proof of Picard's theorem, and Bell's proof of the Painlevé theorem). Analytic Number theory has deep roots in complex analysis. This is illustrated in this volume with the Jacobi's two- and four-square theorems, the Dirichlet prime progression theorem, the prime number theorem, and the Hardy-Littlewood asymptotics for the number of partitions. Two chapters of this book focus on two closely linked subjects: ordinary differential equations with analytic or meromorphic coefficients and asymptotic, especially of integrals. Here we point out the theory of Fuschian differential equations, asymptotic methods (including Euler's method, stationary phase, the saddle-point method, and the WKB method). Univalent functions are discussed in relationship with Schramm-Loewner evolutions, which has become a central part of mathematical analysis. A separate chapter deals with the Nevanlinna theory, which has been developed as an attempt to understand Borel's refinement of Picard's theorem that for finite-order, entire analytic functions, the exponent of convergence of the zeros of \(f(z)-a\) is the same for all values of \(a\) with at most one exception. The chapters on Fuschian differential equations and on asymptotic methods can be viewed as a minicourse on the theory of special functions. Well-chosen exercises illustrate the abstract content of the book. A substantial bibliography of 418 titles is heavily slanted towards applications.

The content is divided into the following six chapters: Riemannian Metrics and Complex Analysis; Some Topics in Analytic Number Theory; Ordinary Differential Equations in the Complex Domain; Asymptotic Methods; Univalent Functions and Loewner Evolution; Nevanlinna Theory.

The book can be recommended as a textbook for complex analysis courses as well as for private reading. This volume provides a comprehensive and macroscopic view of virtually all the fundamental results that relate to the analysis of domains. It is a book that should belong to the personal library of every researcher in complex analysis.
venue: Providence, RI: American Mathematical Society (AMS) (ISBN 978-1-4704-1101-5/hbk). xvi, 321~p. (2015).

title: Harmonic mappings in the plane
abstract:
Harmonic mappings in the plane are univalent functions of a complex variable. Conformal mappings are a special case where the real and imaginary parts are conjugate harmonic functions. Harmonic mappings were studied classically in differential geometry because they provide isothermal parameters for minimal surfaces. More recently, they have been studied by complex analysts. An extensive theory has resulted from the efforts of many mathematicians. It has turned out that numerous results of conformal mapping theory have their counterparts or analogues in this more general setup. Some of these topics are growth and distortion theorems, coefficient problems, and mapping theorems.

This monograph is the first one dedicated to this topic. The exposition is self-contained, only familiarity with complex analysis is required. The usual methods of complex analysis work here, occasionally reinforced with techniques from the theory of minimal surfaces or quasiconformal mappings. Often the results are sharp and geometrically attractive. Some special classes of univalent functions, such as convex functions, have their counterparts here. In this way, classical results of complex analysis find many new applications. As this beautiful monograph shows, this new area has already reached a mature state, but many challenging problems still remain.
venue: Cambridge Tracts in Mathematics 156. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press (ISBN 0-521-64121-7/hbk). xii, 212~p. (2004).

title: Complex versus real analysis. A comparison illustrated by factorization techniques
abstract:
Summary: Comparing real methods with complex ones, sometimes one could have the impression that complex methods are mainly advantageous in special cases. However, in this paper we shall try to show that complex methods can also have high generality, and so they lead to an independent enrichment of real analysis.



 Additionally we discuss some possibilities for getting a unification of high generality of complex and Clifford analysis in relation to systems of partial differential equations.



 Finally we show a feedback of methods of complex and Clifford analysis to the theory of partial differential equations, and some related open problems are discussed. 



 Mainly we discuss \textbf{factorization} techniques, and we try to show that the concept of fundamental solutions is as important for complex and Clifford analysis as it is for real analysis.

For the entire collection see [Zbl 1296.00058].
venue: Le Hung Son (ed.) et al., Interactions between real and complex analysis. Based on the selected lectures of the 20th international conference on finite and infinite dimensional complex analysis and applications (ICFIDCAA), Hanoi, Vietnam, July 29 -- August 3, 2012. Hanoi: Science and Technics Publishing House (ISBN 978-604-67-0032-6/hbk). 414-455 (2012).

title: On a generalization of Mahler's inequality
abstract:
Let \(p_ m(z)=\sum_{j=0}^ m a_ j z^ j\not\equiv 0\) denote a polynomial of degree at most \(n\) with first nonzero coefficient \(a_ N\) that has zeros \((\zeta_ j)_{j=1}^ \nu\) counting multiplicities for \(0<| z|<1\). Then for \(k=0,1,\dots,n\) the relation 
\[
K(p_ m)/\delta_ K(p_ m) \geq 1/\sum_{j=1}^ k {n \choose j}
\]
 holds where \(K(p_ m)=| a_ N| /\prod_{j=0}^ \nu |\zeta_ j| \sum_{j=0}^ m | a_ j|\) and \(\delta_ k(p_ m)=\sum_{j=0}^ k | a_ j| /\sum_{j=0}^ m | a_ j|\). Equality holds for some \(k\geq 1\) only if \(p_ m(z)=\gamma(e^{i\psi}+z)^ n\) with \(\gamma\neq 0\) and \(\psi\in\mathbb{R}\).

title: Complex analysis. In the spirit of Lipman Bers
abstract: This book is based on the original courses of Complex Analysis that was delivered by the well-known American specialist and lecturer in Analysis, Professor Lipman Bers. The book covers most, if not all, of the material on the first year complex analysis, however, it is essentially distinguished from other books devoted to Complex Analysis and its Applications. The authors, as this implies from their Preface to the book, were Lipman Bers's students in Complex Analysis. They have written this course on the base of their notes of Bers's original lectures. The course consists of two parts. The first of them is devoted to the Fundamental Theorem of Complex Analysis; this theorem, due to Lipman Bers, is the statement about the sense of the analyticity property for complex-valued functions of one complex variable from different points of view. The authors formulate this theorem as 8 equivalent, for complex-valued functions of one complex variable, to each other conditions: the usual \({\mathbb C}\)-differentiability of such a function; the Cauchy-Riemann conditions for the corresponding pair of real-valued functions of two variables; the property to have a primitive in each simply connected subdomain in the domain of the complex-valued function of one complex variable under consideration; the Cauchy property to be equal zero for the curve-line integral along a smooth curve bound a simply connected subdomain in the domain of a function under consideration; the validity of Cauchy's integral formula representing a value of the function under consideration at a given point by values of this function on a small circle enclosing the point; the property to be infinitely differentiability; the Weierstrass' property to be a function represented by a Taylor series in a neighborhood of each point in its domain; and, at last, the Runge property of a complex-valued function in one complex variable to be uniformly approximated by rational functions with singularities in some fixed points in the components of the complement to the domain of the complex-valued function of one complex variable under consideration. All these properties and their equivalence are presented in Chapters 1--7: (1) \textsl{The Fundamental Theorem in Complex Function Theory}, (2) \textsl{Foundation}, (3) \textsl{Power Series}, (4) \textsl{The Cauchy Theory -- A Fundamental Theorem}, (5) \textsl{The Cauchy Theory --- Key Consequences}, (6) \textsl{Cauchy Theory: Local Behavior and singularities of Holomorphic Functions}, (7) \textsl{Sequences and Series of Holomorphic Functions}. The other three chapters are devoted to more special problems in Complex Analysis. Chapter 8 \textsl{Conformial Equivalence} deals with fractional linear transformations, the Riemann mapping theorem, the relations between complex-valued functions of one complex variable and hyperbolic (Lobachevskiĭ) geometry and finite Blaschke products. Chapter 9 \textsl{Harmonic functions} deals with integral representations of harmonic functions and the Dirichlet problem, the Mean Value Property and the reflection principle. The last Chapter 10 \textsl{Zeros of Holomorphic Functions} deals with holomorphic functions with prescribed zeros, the field of meromorphic functions and infinite Blaschke products. In the large, the book is written in a clear and easily readable manner. Its merit is a set of 154 exercises that are essentially added and adorned the main body of this book. To my mind, this book is useful for all specialists and lecturers in Complex Analysis (I hope they read this book with pleasure) and also all specialists who deal with applications of Complex Analysis. I also recommend this book to beginners who want to study Complex Analysis, the remarkable part of Mathematics that is full of unexpected, stun, and beauty results with numerous application in Theory of Numbers, Functional Analysis, in Combinatorics and Theory of Groups, Geometry and in many other branches in Mathematics and other Sciences.
venue: Graduate Texts in Mathematics 245. New York, NY: Springer (ISBN 978-0-387-74714-9/hbk). xiii, 220~p. (2007).

title: Fuzzy complex analysis. I: Differentiation
abstract: Since L. A. Zadeh proposed fuzzy sets, many scholars have researched fuzzy calculus such as D. Dubois and Prade. They have carefully discussed fuzzy real numbers and fuzzy real analysis. Of course, it is very important and interesting to study fuzzy complex analysis. The authors of this paper are the first ones to consider fuzzy complex analysis. In this paper, they give the definition of the derivative and discuss some of its elementary properties; research the complex fuzzy numbers and generalized complex fuzzy numbers and discuss the functions mapping real numbers into the set of generalized complex fuzzy numbers. I feel that the fuzzy complex analysis will have the better future of application.

title: The 'pits effect' for the integral function \(f(z) =\sum \exp\{-\rho^{-1} (n\log n-n)+\pi i\alpha n^2\} z^n\), \(\alpha =(\sqrt 5-1)/2\)
abstract:
If \(f_0(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n z^n\) is an integral function of finite non-zero order, consider all the functions \(f\) of the form \(\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \mp a_n z^n\), obtained from \( f_0\) superimposing a ``random factor'' \(\mp 1\). It has long been known that ``almost all'' the functions \(f\) show the ''pits effect'' (briefly: \(| f(z)|\) is exponentially large except in pits of exponentially small area). \textit{M. Nassif} [Proc. Lond. Math. Soc. (2) 54, 201--214 (1952; Zbl 0046.30501)] proved that a factor \( \exp \left(\sqrt{2} \pi i n^{2}\right) \), applied to the integral function of order 2, \( \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{z^{2n}}{n!}\) gets the pits effect. In this paper the author generalizes the result of M. Nassif to the standard functions of finite positive order \(\rho\). He proves that the function 
\[ 
f(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \exp \left\{-k(n(\log n)-n)+\alpha\pi in^2\right\} z^n, \quad k=1/p, \alpha = (\sqrt{5}-1)/2,
\]
has the pits effect; more precisely, if \( p>0, q>0, p+q<1 \) and \( M(r)=\operatorname{Max}_{|z|=q}|f(z)| \), then \(| f(z)|>M^p(r)\), except in circles round the zeros \( z_n\) of radius \(M^q\left(\left|z_n\right|\right)\).
venue: Abh. Zahlentheorie Analysis, zur Erinnerung an E. Landau, 193-215 (1968).

title: Complex analysis
abstract:
This book is an introduction to complex analysis on the undergraduate level and provides a self-study text for students in mathematics and engineering. The plan of the book is fairly traditional, and the material is organized in 13 chapters whose contents we now give in key words. Every chapter contains several exercises. 


Chapter 1. Preliminaries of real analysis: set theory, numbers, sequences and series, functions and continuity, differentiation, integration, improper integrals, calculus of two variables. Chapter 2. Complex numbers: motivation and basic properties of complex numbers. Chapter 3. Prelude to complex analysis: completeness and topology of \(\mathbb C\), functions and continuity. Chapter 4. Differentiation: differentiability, power series, logarithms, singularities. Chapter 5. Complex integration: parametric representation of curves, integration, uniform convergence. 


Chapter 6. Cauchy's theorem. Chapter 7. Consequences of Cauchy's theorem: Cauchy's integral formula, fundamental theorem of algebra, logarithms, Taylor series. Chapter 8. Laurent series and the residue theorem: Laurent series, classification of singularities, residue theorem. Chapter 9. Applications of contour integration: evaluation of real integrals, summation of infinite series.


Chapter 10. Further topics: integration of \(f'/f\), Rouche's theorem, open mapping theorem, winding numbers. Chapter 11. Conformal mappings: preservation of angles, harmonic functions, Möbius transformations. Chapter 12 contains a very brief introduction to Riemann's zeta function and the Riemann hypothesis as well as a descriptive account of iteration, Julia sets and the Mandelbrot set. Chapter 13 gives fully-worked solutions to all exercises.
venue: Springer Undergraduate Mathematics Series. London: Springer. xi, 260 p. EUR 29.95/net; sFr. 51.50; \textsterling 18.95; \$ 34.95 (2003).

title: A course in mathematical analysis. Volume III: Complex analysis, measure and integration
abstract:
This last volume of the series of three volumes of ``A course in mathematical analysis'' covers complex analysis and the theory of measure and integration. The book offers an expanded version of the author's own undergraduate courses taught at the University of Cambridge. The spirit of this volume is clearly developed. The author formulates concepts clearly and explains the relationship between them. The subject matter is important and interesting.

In this volume, the author carefully develops the classical theory of functions of a complex variable. He establishes several basic properties of the complex plane, including a proof of the Jordan curve theorem. A central place is given to the Lebesgue measure, which is used as a model for other measure spaces. Another basic result in this volume is the Radon-Nikodym theorem, in relationship with the differentiation of measures.

The content of this volume is divided into two parts, as follows. Part I is devoted to \textit{Complex analysis} and contains the following seven chapters: Holomorphic functions and analytic functions; The topology of the complex plane; Complex integration; Zeros and singularities; The calculus of residues; Conformal transformations; Applications. Part II deals with \textit{Measure and integration} and contains the following eight chapters: Lebesgue measure on \({\mathbb R}\); Measurable spaces and measurable functions; Integration; Constructing measures; Signed measures and complex measures; Measures on metric spaces; Differentiation; Applications. Over 250 exercises and numerous examples and applications challenge the reader to learn through practice and illustrate the abstract results in this volume.

This is a very beautiful book, coming at the right moment. It is an intuitively motivated, original, and insightful presentation of many topics in classical complex analysis and related areas. The volume is a remarkable mix of classical results and research topics at the forefront of actual research. It is full of cross fertilizations of different theories and will be useful to undergraduate and graduate students, as well as to many researchers in other fields (functional analysis, partial differential equations, mathematical physics).

title: Basic complex analysis. A comprehensive course in analysis, part 2A
abstract:
This is the second volume of a a five volume set of books that is a graduate analysis text. The theory of functions of one complex variable is an exciting field and a privileged meeting place of many important branches of mathematics, and each one of them could claim ownership of this sparkling jewel. Complex analysis had always a privileged position in the education of a mathematics student. The book under review is a modern take on the subject, with the explicit goal of highlighting the geometric intuitions underlying the theory of functions.

This volume is devoted to the basic complex analysis and its content is divided into the following chapters: The Cauchy integral theorem: Basics; Consequences of the Cauchy integral formula; Chains and the ultimate Cauchy integral theorem; More consequences of the Cauchy integral theorem; Spaces of analytic functions; Fractional linear transformations; Conformal maps; Zeros of analytic functions and product formulae; Elliptic functions; Selected additional topics. Selected topics of this volume include the Goursat argument, ultimate and ultra Cauchy integral formulae, Runge's theorem, complex interpolation, Marty's theorem, continued fraction analysis of real numbers, Riemann mapping theorem, uniformization theorem, Mittag Leffler and Weirstrass product theorems, Hadamard product formula, Gamma function, Euler-Maclaurin series and Stirling's formula, Blaschke products, Weierstrass and Jacobi elliptic functions, Jacobi theta functions, Paley-Wiener theorems, Hartog's phenomenon.

The book could be used for self-study or in a graduate course. This reviewer considers that this is a very useful book, which is readable, elegant, pedagogically written and illustrated by an excellent choice of exercises. It will also serve well as a reference to professionals and researchers in various fields related to complex analysis.
venue: Providence, RI: American Mathematical Society (AMS) (ISBN 978-1-4704-1100-8/hbk). xviii, 641~p. (2015).

title: On a counterexample in the theory of polynomials having concentration at low degrees
abstract:
A complex polynomial \(p(z)= \sum^m_{j=0} a_jz^j\) is said to have concentration \(d\) at degree \(k\) if 
\[
\sum^k_{j=0} |a_j |\geq d \sum^m_{j=0} |a_j |).\tag{*}
\]
 Let \(J(p) = \int^1_0 \log |p(e^{2\pi it}) |dt - \log \left(\sum^m_{j=0} |a_j |\right)\) and let \(C_{d,k}\) be the infimum of \(J(p)\) over all polynomials satisfying \((*)\). It had been conjectured that this infimum is attained by a Hurwitz polynomial, i.e. one with real coefficients and with all zeros in \(\text{Re} z <0\) [\textit{A. K. Rigler}, \textit{S. Y. Trimble} and \textit{R. S. Varga}, Rocky Mt. J. Math. 19, No. 1, 353-373 (1989; Zbl 0692.30002)]. This paper disproves the conjecture and indeed shows that there are infinitely many counterexamples.

title: Non-trivial entire solutions of the functional equation \(f(\lambda)+f(\omega \lambda)f(\omega ^{-1}\lambda)=1\), \(\omega ^ 5=1\)
abstract:
The author and \textit{R. H. Cameron} [Sympos. Ordinary Differential Equations, Minneapolis, Minnesota, 1972, Lect. Notes Math. 312, 194-202 (1973; Zbl 0256.30003)] proved that a Stokes multiplier of the differential equation 
\[
 d^ 2y/dx^ 2-(x^ 3+\lambda)y=0 
\]
 yields a nontrivial entire solution of the functional equation \(f(\lambda)+f(\omega \lambda)f(\omega^{-1}\lambda)=1\), \(\omega =\exp (2\pi i/5)\) such that \(f(0)=\omega^ 2+\omega^{-2}\). In this paper, the author constructs two nontrivial entire functions of this functional equation such that \(f(0)=\omega +\omega^{-1}\), by using a Stokes multiplier of the differential equation \(y''-(x^{1/2}+\lambda)y=0\) and a Stokes multiplier of the differential equation \(y''-(x^{-1/3}+\lambda x^{-2/3})y=0\). The author shows in two theorems that these two solutions exhibit different asymptotic behaviours as \(\lambda\) \(\to \infty\).

title: Differential forms on singular varieties. De Rham and Hodge theory simplified
abstract:
This book offers a novel and complete treatment of the Deligne theory of mixed Hodge structures on the cohomology of singular spaces. The cohomology of a singular complex space carries a mixed Hodge structure defined using complexes of differential forms, wherein the weight filtrations of Deligne and Grothendieck induces a graduation on the cohomology, and each quotient space of the graduation carries a pure Hodge structure. It is a delicate affair to explain the mixed Hodge structure for the cohomology of a singular space, although it is relatively easy for an algebraic manifold. 

The book is divided into three parts: Part I: ``Classical Hodge Theory'' (seven chapters); Part II: ``Differential forms on complex spaces'' (three chapters); Part III: ``Mixed Hodge structures on noncompact spaces'' (four chapters). 

Part I outlines the necessary prerequisites from the theory of spectral sequences, the theory of complex manifolds and vector bundles, cohomology of sheaves, harmonic forms, Kählerian manifolds, residues on a smooth divisor, and complex spaces. This makes the exposition relatively self-contained. 

Part II treats differential forms on complex spaces. The tools developed are used to construct mixed Hodge structures on compact complex spaces in Part II and on noncompact complex spaces in Part III. Due to the complicated machinery involved, the book is not easily accessible. It is, nevertheless, well-written for an advanced level text and it is valuable to beginning researchers as an introduction to an important and beautiful mathematical subject. The book will also serve as a good reference for further research work.
venue: Pure and Applied Mathematics (Boca Raton) 273. Boca Raton, FL: Chapman \& Hall/CRC (ISBN 0-8493-3739-9/hbk). xix, 312~p. (2006).

title: The ultimate companion to ``A comprehensive course in analysis. A five-volume reference set''
abstract:
The five-volume set ``A comprehensive course in analysis'' is an excellent graduate-level analysis material containing not only abstract results but also hundreds of problems and numerous notes that extend the text and provide important historical background.

 The content is divided into four parts that include five volumes. These volumes will be reviewed individually and are titled, respectively, \textit{Real Analysis} (Part~1) [Zbl 1332.00003], \textit{Basic Complex Analysis} and \textit{Advanced Complex Analysis} (Parts~2A and~2B) [Zbl 1332.00004 and Zbl 1332.00005], \textit{Harmonic Analysis} (Part~3) [Zbl 1334.00002], and \textit{Operator Theory} (Part~4) [Zbl 1334.00003]. 



The item under review contains the table of contents of the five volumes together with prefaces of all parts. A sample section (Section~3.5 in Part~1) devoted to \textit{Classical Fourier Series} is also included.



Editorial remark:
This item is a brochure produced for promotional purposes.
venue: Providence, RI: American Mathematical Society (AMS). vi, 133~p. (2015).

title: Reproducing kernels in complex analysis
abstract:
Summary: This paper discusses the role of reproducing kernels in complex analysis, both in one and several complex variables. We give applications to the study of the boundary smoothness of biholomorphic mappings and to other parts of geometric analysis. We develop the theme of constructible kernels versus non-constructible kernels and compare and contrast the two.

For the entire collection see [Zbl 1495.30001].
venue: Krantz, Steven G. (ed.), Handbook of complex analysis. Boca Raton, FL: CRC Press. 379-425 (2022).

title: Linear and complex analysis for applications
abstract:
``Linear and Complex Analysis for Applications'' provides the reader with a deep appreciation of linear analysis and complex analysis and how these subjects fit into mathematics. The main goals of this volume are the following: (i) to develop enough linear analysis and complex variable theory to prepare students in engineering or applied mathematics for advanced work; (ii) to unify many distinct and seemingly isolated topics; (iii) to show mathematics as both interesting and useful, especially via the juxtaposition of examples and theorems.

In the first part of this volume, the author provides some basic results of linear algebra, complex numbers, as well as topics from calculus III. The material on linear algebra includes infinite-dimensional examples arising from elementary calculus and differential equations. Line and surface integrals are computed both in the language of classical vector analysis and by using differential forms. Interesting connections between various topics and applications appear throughout the book.

The content of this book is divided into the following seven chapters: 1. Linear algebra; 2. Complex numbers; 3. Vector analysis; 4. Complex analysis; 5. Transform methods; 6. Hilbert spaces; 7. Examples and applications. There is also an appendix devoted to the language of mathematics. All the topics are motivated very nicely, and there are many exercises, which make the book ideal for a course on the subject. The style is concise, always very neat, and proofs are given with full details. Hence, I certainly suggest this nice textbook to anyone interested in the subject, even for self-study.
venue: Advances in Applied Mathematics (Boca Raton). Boca Raton, FL: CRC Press (ISBN 978-1-4987-5610-5/hbk; 978-1-032-47702-2/pbk; 978-1-315-11884-0/ebook). x, 264~p. (2017).

title: Genetic algorithms and Walsh functions. I: A gentle introduction
abstract:
The paper investigates the application of Walsh functions to the analysis of genetic algorithms and introduces Bethke's Walsh-schema transform through the Walsh polynomials. The introduction of a polynomial form presents a common language for discussing the usual composition of mappings that occurs in genetic algorithms when coding are decoded, mapped, and passed as parameters to some objective function. This form provides an intuitive basis for visualizing the interactions of functions and codings using straightforward algebraic methods. The use of polynomials makes proofs and algebraic manipulation more direct and intuitive.


The Walsh functions are presented in monomial form and fitness functions are written as a weighted sum of the Walsh functions.


The analysis techniques are illustrated by several specific examples, which have demonstrated the usefulness of the methods in determining whether particular coding-function combinations should be easy or hard for a genetic algorithm. A method has been used to design fully deceptive problems. Pascal code implementing the fast Walsh transform is given as an appendix to the paper.

title: Invitation to complex analysis
abstract:
This book is a nice introduction to complex analysis with some interesting and unusual features. The volume under review is ideal for a first course in complex analysis, but it can be used either as a classroom text or for independent study. The treatment goes beyond the standard material of power series, Cauchy's theorem, residues, conformal mapping, and harmonic functions by including accessible discussions of intriguing topics that are uncommon in a book at this level. The flexibility afforded by the supplementary topics and applications makes the book adaptable either to a short, one-term course or to a comprehensive, full-year course.

The content of the book is divided into five chapters, as follows: 1. From Complex Numbers to Cauchy's Theorem; 2. Applications of Cauchy's Theorem; 3. Analytic Continuation; 4. Harmonic Functions and Conformal Mappings; 5. Miscellaneous Topics.

Some of the features of this book are the following: (i) the volume is written in an informal style by a master expositor; (ii) the book distills more than half a century of experience in an active research field; (iii) it deals with basic notions in complex analysis but also discusses additional interesting topics; (iv) detailed solutions of the exercises both serve as models for students and facilitate independent study; (v) supplementary exercises, not solved in the book, provide an additional teaching tool; (vi) the topics are discussed in commonly encountered terms, rather than in a general, abstract setting.

This volume can be used either as a classroom text or for independent study. The book reveals both the power of complex analysis as a tool for applications and the intrinsic beauty of the subject as a fundamental part of pure mathematics. The book under review is strongly recommended to advanced undergraduates and beginning graduate students, but is also very suitable for readers acquainted with advanced calculus or introductory real analysis.
venue: MAA Textbooks. Washington, DC: Mathematical Association of America (MAA) (ISBN 978-0-88385-764-9/hbk). xiv, 327~p. (2010).

title: Dynamical processes on complex networks.
abstract:
The availability of large data sets has allowed researchers to uncover complex properties such as large-scale fluctuations and heterogeneities in many networks, leading to the breakdown of standard theoretical frameworks and models. Until recently these systems were considered as haphazard sets of points and connections. 

Recent advances have generated a vigorous research effort in understanding the effect of complex connectivity patterns on dynamical phenomena. This book presents a comprehensive account of these effects. 

The book consists of 13 chapters, an index and a nice bibliography (more than 400 references). 

Chapter 1 defines the basics of graph theory and some tools for the statistical characterization and classification of large networks. 

These tools are used in Chapter 2 to discuss the complex features which characterize a large number of real-world networks. 

Chapter 3 is devoted to a brief presentation of the various network modeling techniques and the general approaches used for their analysis. Approximations and computational techniques are discussed here as well.

Chapter 4 provides an introduction to the basic theoretical concepts and tools needed for the analysis of dynamical systems taking place on networks.

Chapter 5 reports the analysis of equilibrium processes and reviews the behaviors of basic equilibrium physical models such as the Ising model in complex networks.

Chapter 6 addresses the analysis of damage and attack processes in complex networks by mapping those processes into percolation phase transitions.

Chapter 7 is devoted to synchronization in coupled systems with complex connectivity patterns.

The following four chapters of the book are devoted to non-equilibrium processes, such as search, navigation, exploration, epidemic spreading, the emergence of social behavior, traffic avalanche and congestion.

Chapter 12 discusses the recent application of network science to biological systems.

The final Chapter 13 contains appendices to detail very technical calculations used in the book.

This book will be useful for graduate students and researchers in dynamical processes on complex networks.

title: Complex analysis
abstract:
The author of this fine textbook is a prominent function theorist. He leads the reader in a careful but far-sighted way from the elements of complex analysis to advanced topics in function theory and at some points to topics of modern research. Geometric aspects (the Riemannian point of view) and purely analytic aspects (the Weierstrass point of view) are treated in a balanced way, figures appear frequently in the text.


Part I of the book (7 chapters, 223 pages) contains basic material up to Laurent series and the residue calculus. Functions like \(\sqrt z\), \(\exp(z)\), \(\sin(z)\), \(\sqrt{z(1-z)}\) including a `light' discussion of their Riemann surfaces appear already in Chapter I. Line integrals and complex integration are treated via their real-analytic counterparts. In particular, Green's theorems are used freely. There are also sections on fluid dynamics and other applications. Doubly periodic functions and complex Fourier series are part of Chapter VI, and in Chapter VII Cauchy principal value integrals are treated besides the standard topics of the residue calculus.


Part II (4 chapters, 90 pages) begins with the argument principle and Rouché's theorem, followed by the `jump theorem' and a thorough discussion of simple connectivity. The Schwarz lemma, the Schwarz reflection principle, and the Schwarz-Christoffel formula follow in Chapters IX, X and XI. Included in Chapter IX are the basic notions of hyperbolic geometry in the unit disk. Compact families are introduced with the Riemann mapping theorem in view, which is proved at the end of Part II.


Part III (5 chapters, 131 pages) is very rich in advanced topics. Chapter XII begins with a thorough treatment of compact families of meromorphic functions. The key theorem is Marty's theorem which requires the uniform boundedness of the spherical derivatives on compact subsets. Zalcman's lemma and Montel's theorem (3 values omitted) give criteria for normality. Picard's theorems (big and little) follow. The remainder of Chapter XII (3 sections) gives an ab ovo introduction into modern iteration theory of rational functions. Fatou sets, Julia sets, basin of attraction, periodic points are some of the topics. In particular, one is interested in the connectedness of the Julia set. The iteration of quadratic polynomials \(P(z)= z^2+ c\) leads to the Mandelbrot set \(M\). Runge's approximation theorem and some of its applications are in Chapter XIII, followed by the theorem of Mittag-Leffler and Weierstrass. The highlight of Chapter XIV is the prime number theorem proved via a Tauberian theorem for Laplace integrals. Dirichlet series come along the way. In Chapter XV the general Dirichlet problem is discussed and solved via the Perron method, and Riemann's mapping theorem is proved once more. Also treated are Green's function and harmonic measure.


In the last chapter a careful introduction to Riemann surfaces is given, the Dirichlet problem and the general Green's function are studied as well as the bipolar Green's function. All this culminates in Koebe's uniformization theorem: Every simply connected Riemann surface is conformally equivalent to either one of \(\mathbb{C}\), \(\mathbb{C}^*\) or \(\mathbb{D}\).


At the beginning of the book there are two pages of historical remarks, just enough for a text on complex analysis. We found, however, that a special feature of the book is the wealth of exercises at the end of each section, ranging from easy and straightforward ones to demanding ones which sometimes prepare the ground for later developments. Hints for solutions are given for many of the exercises.


Altogether, the author has given us a wonderful textbook for use in classroom and in seminars.
venue: Undergraduate Texts in Mathematics. New York, NY: Springer. xvi, 478 p. (2001).

title: Universal approximation properties of overconvergent power series on open sets
abstract: Let \({\mathcal O}\subset {\mathbb{C}}\) be an open set with simply connected components. The author proves the existence of a holomorphic function f in \({\mathcal O}\) with the following properties. Denote by \(p_ n=p_ n(z)\) the sequence of partial sums of the Taylor expansion of f at a point \(z_ 0\in {\mathcal O}\). Then: 1. Some subsequence \(p_{n_ k}\) converges to f in \({\mathcal O}\). 2. For every compact \(B\subset {\mathbb{C}}\setminus \bar {\mathcal O}\) which does not separate the plane and any function g which is continuous on B and holomorphic in its interior there exists a subsequence \(p_{n_ k}\) converging to g. The set of such ''universal'' functions is dense in the space of all holomorphic functions in \({\mathcal O}\).

abstract: Summary: We study the Carathéodory and Kobayashi metrics by way of the method of dual extremal problems in functional analysis. Particularly, incisive results are obtained for convex domains.

title: Solving real linear systems with the complex Schur decomposition
abstract: An algorithm is presented that makes use of complex Schur decomposition to solve a linear system. Backward error analysis is used to show that the algorithm produces a complex solution that solves a nearby, but complex, linear system. It is proved that when a real linear system is subjected to complex perturbations, then the real part of the solution to the perturbed system solves a nearby real linear system. The proposed algorithm is useful, because, in a variety of cases one cannot work with the real Schur form without greatly increasing the volume of work.
venue: SIAM J. Matrix Anal. Appl. 29, No. 1, 177-183 (2006).

title: Function theory of one complex variable.
abstract:
The book whose first edition was published in 1997 is very well written, it is extremely clear and very detailed. It is suitable to be used as a textbook in a first-year graduate course in complex analysis, as it covers all material usually treated in such a course. In addition to this, the book includes a number of special topics such as Hardy spaces, the Bergman kernel function and the Bell-Ligocka approach to proving smoothness to the boundary of biholomorphic mappings. 

One of the features of the book is that it emphasizes the connection between real and complex analysis. The authors assert in the preface to the first edition that the easiest path into complex analysis is to observe how at least its rudiments arise directly from ideas about calculus which with the student must be familiar. Consequently, they pursue this point of view both by comparing and by constrasting complex variable theory with real-variable claculus.

We should also mention that the distinct chapters or sections include very nice introductions which illuminate the subjects involved from several sides. 

The book contains many solved examples and a good number of exercises as an essential part of it. In this edition, many of the exercises have been revised and in some cases rearranged for consistency. Also a considerable number of the proofs, especially in the later chapters, have been corrected, clarified, or simplified.

I can say that I have read this book with great pleasure and I do recommend it for those who are interested in complex analysis.
venue: Graduate Studies in Mathematics 40. Providence, RI: American Mathematical Society (AMS) (ISBN 0-8218-3962-4/hbk). xix, 504~p. (2006).

title: Complex analysis. The hitchhiker's guide to the plane
abstract:
The book under review is the second edition of the quite successful 1983 edition [Zbl 0501.30001]. The first edition was an engaging and clear treatment of the topics covered in a typical first introduction to complex analysis meant for a mathematics undergraduate student. Some nice highlights of the presentation in the first edition were frequent comparisons of the complex analysis results with real analysis ones, the role of the geometry and topology of the plane, and historical discussions. The audience for the new edition is the same, and book retains the aforementioned pleasant traits of the first edition.

The new edition has substantial additions, expanding the book size from the earlier 290 pages to a much lengthier book of 389 pages.

Although it does not compare favourably with the older version in terms of conciseness, the additions are quite interesting, giving the book a niche among the many textbooks on the subject. In particular, there is an added chapter on a nonstandard analysis treatment of infinitesimals and a discussion of space filling curves. While covering the entire book in a short undergraduate course might not be feasible, there is on the other hand ample material in the book for designing interesting teacher-led student projects for deeper understanding and for self-study for gifted students. In conclusion, this is an excellent undergraduate book on complex analysis and is highly recommended both as a textbook for a course and as an addition to libraries for supplementary reading alongside a course on complex analysis.

title: A very complicated proof of the minimax theorem
abstract: Summary: The justly celebrated von Neumann minimax theorem has many proofs. Here I reproduce the most complex one I am aware of. This provides a fine didactic example for many courses in convex analysis or functional analysis.
