	  <jats:p>It is shown from the form of the disturbance equation that there is an upper limit to the value of <jats:italic>n</jats:italic> (≠ 0) for a neutral (inviscid) disturbance with <jats:italic>c</jats:italic><jats:sub>
	      <jats:italic>r</jats:italic>
	    </jats:sub> equal to the value of <jats:italic>U</jats:italic> at the point where Q′ = 0. In the case of a jet with a ‘far-downstream’ profile, only the value <jats:italic>n</jats:italic> = 1 satisfies this restriction; thus only the sinuous mode <jats:italic>n</jats:italic> = 1 can yield amplified disturbances in an inviscid fluid. A numerical investigation shows that for this profile the wave-number of the neutral disturbance with <jats:italic>n</jats:italic> = 1 is α = 1·46.</jats:p>

title: Some specific features of atmospheric turbulence
abstract: <jats:p>The spectrum of atmospheric turbulence is very broad by comparison with spectra in wind tunnels. We introduce the notion of small-scale and large-scale turbulence. Small-scale turbulence consists of a set of disturbances, the scales of which do not exceed the distance to the wall and for which the hypothesis of three-dimensional isotropy is valid in a certain rough approximation. Large-scale turbulence is essentially anisotropic; the horizontal scale in the atmosphere is much larger than the vertical one, the latter being confined to a certain characteristic height <jats:italic>H</jats:italic>. The horizontal scale varies widely according to the external conditions and characteristics of the medium.</jats:p>

title: The large-scale structure of homogeneous turbulence
abstract: <jats:p>A field of homogeneous turbulence generated at an initial instant by a distribution of random impulsive forces is considered. The statistical properties of the forces are assumed to be such that the integral moments of the cumulants of the force system all exist. The motion generated has the property that at the initial instant<jats:disp-formula id="S0022112067000552_frm1">\[ E(\kappa) = C\kappa^2 + o(\kappa^2), \]</jats:disp-formula>where<jats:italic>E</jats:italic>(<jats:italic>k</jats:italic>) is the energy spectrum function,<jats:italic>k</jats:italic>is the wave-number magnitude, and<jats:italic>C</jats:italic>is a positive number which is not in general zero. The corresponding forms of the velocity covariance spectral tensor and correlation tensor are determined. It is found that the terms in the velocity covariance<jats:italic>R</jats:italic><jats:sub><jats:italic>ij</jats:italic></jats:sub>(<jats:bold>r</jats:bold>) are<jats:italic>O</jats:italic>(<jats:italic>r</jats:italic><jats:sup>−3</jats:sup>) for large values of the separation magnitude<jats:italic>r</jats:italic>.</jats:p><jats:p>An argument based on the conservation of momentum is used to show that<jats:italic>C</jats:italic>is a dynamical invariant and that the forms of the velocity covariance at large separation and the spectral tensor at small wave number are likewise invariant. For isotropic turbulence, the Loitsianski integral diverges but the integral<jats:disp-formula id="S0022112067000552_frm2">\[ \int_0^{\infty} r^2R(r)dr = \frac{1}{2}\pi C \]</jats:disp-formula>exists and is invariant.</jats:p>

title: On the oscillatory instability of a differentially heated fluid loop
abstract: <jats:p>A theoretical discussion is given of the motion of a fluid contained in a tube forming a closed loop that is heated from below and cooled from above. The fluid is assumed to have uniform temperature over each cross-section, and the heat transfer is assumed proportional to the difference between the local temperatures of the fluid and the tube. The latter temperature is prescribed. The system has one steady solution with warm fluid rising in one branch and cold fluid sinking in the other. This solution may, however, become unstable in an oscillatory manner. A weak instability takes the form of pulsations, the motion being always of one sign, while a strong instability takes the form of oscillations with zero mean motion. These oscillations are irregular and do not repeat themselves even over very long times.</jats:p><jats:p>These unstable motions are associated with thermal anomalies in the fluid that are advected materially around the loop. The anomalies amplify through the correlated variations in flow rate. A warm pocket of fluid creates maximum flow rate going through the upper part and minimum flow rate going through the lower part of the loop. Accordingly it passes quicker through the heat sink than through the heat source, and the latter becomes more effective. Similarly, the heat sink acts more effectively on a cold pocket of fluid.</jats:p><jats:p>The curve of neutral stability is worked out as a function of the two parameters of the problem, a non-dimensional gravity and a non-dimensional friction coefficient. The instability has also been studied by direct numerical time integration of the model equations.</jats:p><jats:p>It is suggested that the mechanism of instability found for this model operates also in more complicated systems, and can explain the pulsative type of motions observed recently in certain convection experiments.</jats:p>

title: Gravity currents and related phenomena
abstract:
<jats:p>This paper presents a broad investigation into the properties of steady gravity currents, in so far as they can be represented by perfect-fluid theory and simple extensions of it (like the classical theory of hydraulic jumps) that give a rudimentary account of dissipation. As usually understood, a gravity current consists of a wedge of heavy fluid (e.g. salt water, cold air) intruding into an expanse of lighter fluid (fresh water, warm air); but it is pointed out in § 1 that, if the effects of viscosity and mixing of the fluids at the interface are ignored, the hydrodynamical problem is formally the same as that for an empty cavity advancing along the upper boundary of a liquid. Being simplest in detail, the latter problem is treated as a prototype for the class of physical problems under study: most of the analysis is related to it specifically, but the results thus obtained are immediately applicable to gravity currents by scaling the gravitational constant according to a simple rule.</jats:p>
	  <jats:p>In § 2 the possible states of steady flow in the present category between fixed horizontal boundaries are examined on the assumption that the interface becomes horizontal far downstream. A certain range of flows appears to be possible when energy is dissipated; but in the absence of dissipation only one flow is possible, in which the asymptotic level of the interface is midway between the plane boundaries. The corresponding flow in a tube of circular cross-section is found in § 3, and the theory is shown to be in excellent agreement with the results of recent experiments by Zukoski. A discussion of the effects of surface tension is included in § 3. The two-dimensional energy-conserving flow is investigated further in § 4, and finally a close approximation to the shape of the interface is obtained. In § 5 the discussion turns to the question whether flows characterized by periodic wavetrains are realizable, and it appears that none is possible without a large loss of energy occurring. In § 6 the case of infinite total depth is considered, relating to deeply submerged gravity currents. It is shown that the flow must always feature a breaking ‘head wave’, and various properties of the resulting wake are demonstrated. Reasonable agreement is established with experimental results obtained by Keulegan and others.</jats:p>

title: Standing waves on a contracting or expanding current
abstract:
<jats:p>In a recent work Longuet-Higgins & Stewart (1961) have studied the changes in wavelength and amplitude of progressive waves of constant frequency as they are propagated into regions of surface divergence or convergence. In the work here described the complementary conditions are assumed. Standing waves of uniform wavelength, λ, exist in an area of uniform surface divergence. Changes in amplitude and wavelength are studied. These changes depend on the existence of the radiation stress which was discovered by Longuet-Higgins & Stewart but the physical interpretation of this stress is simpler for standing than for progressive waves. Three different ways of obtaining the same rate of strain in the direction of the current caused the amplitude to vary as <jats:inline-formula>$\lambda|^{-{\frac {1}{4}}}, \lambda|^{-{\frac {3}{4}}}$</jats:inline-formula> and <jats:inline-formula>$\lambda|^{-{\frac {5}{4}}}$</jats:inline-formula>, respectively.</jats:p>
	  <jats:p>Experiments in which free-standing waves were generated in a tank one wave-length wide which was then made narrower verified the conclusion that contraction does not alter the periodic character of the waves, even though the ratio of amplitude to wavelength becomes so great that they can no longer be treated mathematically by the usual linearized approximation. The shape of the profile then appears to agree well with calculations of Penney & Price (1952).</jats:p>

title: On the laminar flow in a free jet of liquid at high Reynolds numbers
abstract: <jats:p>This paper is concerned with the jet of liquid, open to the atmosphere, that emerges from a two-dimensional channel in which there is Poiseuille flow far upstream, the flow being driven by an applied pressure gradient. The problem is discussed with the aid of the method of matched asymptotic expansions; the small parameter involved is the inverse Reynolds number. A boundary layer forms adjacent to the free surface, and a classical boundary-layer analysis is applied to find the flow there (for moderate distances downstream); the influence of this boundary layer on the flow in the core of the jet is then investigated. Higher-order boundary-layer effects, such as indeterminacy and eigensolutions, are also discussed. The first few terms are found of an asymptotic expansion for the equation of the free surface, and considerations of momentum balance are applied to find the asymptotic contraction ratio of the jet.</jats:p>

title: Temperature fluctuations in the atmospheric boundary layer
abstract: <jats:p>A systematic analysis is made of published measurements of the magnitude of temperature fluctuations in the atmospheric boundary layer. These cover a wide range of height, wind speed, and thermal stratification. Within appropriate ranges of the variables, there is evidence for the existence of a dominantly forced convection régime, and also one wherein the predictions of the similarity theory of free convection are fairly closely approached. Subject to the limitations set by the recording systems used, regression relations are obtained for the magnitude of the fluctuations in terms of height and vertical temperature gradient or heat flux.</jats:p>

title: Numerical integration of the three-dimensional Navier-Stokes equations for incompressible flow
abstract: <jats:p>A method of numerically integrating the Navier-Stokes equations for certain three-dimensional incompressible flows is described. The technique is presented through application to the particular problem of describing thermal convection in a rotating annulus. The equations, in cylindrical polar co-ordinate form, are integrated with respect to time by a marching process, together with the solving of a Poisson equation for the pressure. A suitable form of the finite difference equations gives a computationally-stable long-term integration with reasonably faithful representation of the spatial and temporal characteristics of the flow.</jats:p><jats:p>Trigonometric interpolation techniques provide accurate (discretely exact) solutions to the Poisson equation. By using an auxiliary algorithm for rapid evaluation of trigonometric transforms, the proportion of computation needed to solve the Poisson equation can be reduced to less than 25% of the total time needed to’ advance one time step. Computing on a UNIVAC 1108 machine, the flow can be advanced one time-step in 2 sec for a 14 × 14 × 14 grid upward to 96 sec for a 60 × 34 × 34 grid.</jats:p><jats:p>As an example of the method, some features of a solution for steady wave flow in annulus convection are presented. The resemblance of this flow to the classical Eady wave is noted.</jats:p>

title: On the stability of steady finite amplitude convection
abstract: <jats:p>The static state of a horizontal layer of fluid heated from below may become unstable. If the layer is infinitely large in horizontal extent, the Boussinesq equations admit many different steady solutions. A systematic method is presented here which yields the finite-amplitude steady solutions by means of successive approximations. It turns out that not every solution of the linear problem is an approximation to the non-linear problem, yet there are still an infinite number of finite amplitude solutions. A similar procedure has been applied to the stability problem for these steady finite amplitude solutions with the result that three-dimensional solutions are unstable but there is a class of two-dimensional flows which are stable. The problem has been treated for both rigid and free boundaries.</jats:p>

title: On the non-linear mechanics of hydrodynamic stability
abstract: <jats:p>In most work on the theory of stability of laminar flow, infinitesimal disturbances only have been considered, so that only the <jats:italic>initial</jats:italic> growth of the disturbance has been determined. It is the object of the present paper to extend the theory to larger amplitudes and to study the mechanics of disturbance growth with the inherent non-linearity of the hydrodynamical system taken into account.</jats:p><jats:p>The Reynolds stress (where averages are taken with respect to some suitable space coordinate) is the fundamental consequence of the non-linearity, and its effects can be anticipated as follows. Initially a disturbance grows exponentially with time according to the linear theory, but eventually it reaches such a size that the transport of momentum by the finite fluctuations is appreciable and the associated mean stress (the Reynolds stress) then has an appreciable effect on the mean flow. This distortion of the mean flow modifies the rate of transfer of energy from the mean flow to the disturbance and, since this energy transfer is the cause of the growth of the disturbance, there is a modification of the rate of growth of the latter.</jats:p><jats:p>It is suggested that, in many cases, an equilibrium state may be possible in which the rate of transfer of energy from the (distorted) mean flow to the disturbance balances precisely the rate of viscous dissipation of the energy of disturbance. A theory based on certain assumptions about the energy flow is given to describe both the growth of the disturbance and the final equilibrium state, and application is made to the cases of Poiseuille flow between parallel planes and flow between rotating cylinders. The distorted mean flow in the equilibrium state can be calculated and from this, in the latter case, the torque required to maintain the cylinders in motion. Good agreement is obtained with G. I. Taylor's measurements of the torque for the case when the inner cylinder rotates and the outer cylinder is at rest.</jats:p>

title: Deposition of a viscous fluid on a plane surface
abstract: <jats:p>Two mechanisms by which a viscous fluid can be deposited on a plane surface are described. Measurement of the thickness of the deposit are compared with calculated values. It is found that the two agree within rather wide limits of experimental error provided the effect of surface tension can be neglected, and the conditions under which this is legitimate are discussed.</jats:p>

title: Small-scale variation of convected quantities like temperature in turbulent fluid. II: The case of large conductivity
abstract:
<jats:p>The analysis reported in Part 1 is extended here to the case in which the conductivity κ is large compared with the viscosity ν, the conduction ‘cut-off’ to the θ-spectrum then being at wave-number (ε/κ<jats:sup>3</jats:sup>)<jats:sup>¼</jats:sup>. It is shown, with a plausible and consistent hypothesis, that the convective supply of <jats:inline-formula>$\overline {\theta^2}$</jats:inline-formula>-stuff to Fourier components of θ with wave-numbers <jats:italic>n</jats:italic> in the range (ε/κ<jats:sup>3</jats:sup>)<jats:sup>¼</jats:sup> [Lt      ] <jats:italic>n</jats:italic> [Gt      ] (ε/ν<jats:sup>3</jats:sup>)<jats:sup>¼</jats:sup> is due primarily to motion on a length scale of order <jats:italic>n</jats:italic><jats:sup>-1</jats:sup> acting on a uniform gradient of θ of magnitude <jats:inline-formula>$[(\overline {\nabla \theta)^2}]^{\frac {1}{2}}$</jats:inline-formula>. The consequent form of the theta;-spectrum within this same wave-number range is
<jats:inline-formula>$\Gamma (n) = \frac {1}{3}C \chi \epsilon ^{\frac {2}{3}} k^{-3}n^ {-\frac {17} {3}}.$</jats:inline-formula></jats:p>
	  <jats:p>The way in which conduction influences (and restricts) the effect of convection on the distribution of θ at these wave-numbers beyond the conduction cut-off is discussed.</jats:p>

title: The three-dimensional nature of boundary-layer instability
abstract: <jats:p>An experimental investigation is described in which principal emphasis is given to revealing the nature of the motions in the non-linear range of boundary-layer instability and the onset of turbulence. It has as its central purpose the evaluation of existing theoretical considerations and the provision of a sound physical model which can be taken as a basis for a theoretical approach. The experimental method consisted of introducing, in a two-dimensional boundary layer on a flat plate at ‘incompressible’ speeds, three-dimensional disturbances under controlled conditions using the vibrating-ribbon technique, and studying their growth and evolution using hot-wire methods. It has been definitely established that longitudinal vortices are associated with the non-linear three-dimensional wave motions. Sufficient data were obtained for an evaluation of existing theoretical approaches. Those which have been considered are the generation of higher harmonics, the interaction of the mean flow and the Reynold stress, the concave streamline curvature associated with the wave motion, the vortex loop and the non-linear effect of a three-dimensional perturbation. It appears that except for the latter they do not adequately describe the observed phenomena. It is not that they are incorrect or may not play a role in some aspect of the local behaviour, but from the over-all point of view the results suggest that it is the non-linear effect of a three-dimensional perturbation which dominates the behaviour. A principal conclusion to be drawn is that a new perspective, one that takes three-dimensionality into account, is required in connexion with boundary-layer instability. It is demonstrated that the actual breakdown of the wave motion into turbulence is a consequence of a new instability which arises in the aforementioned three-dimensional wave motion. This instability involves the generation of ‘hairpin’ eddies and is remarkably similar in behaviour to ‘inflexional’ instability. It is also shown that the results obtained from the study of controlled disturbances are equally applicable to ‘natural’ transition.</jats:p>

title: Internal waves of finite amplitude and permanent form
abstract: <jats:p>A theory is derived for the class of long two-dimensional waves, comprising solitary and periodic cnoidal waves, that can propagate with unchanging form in heterogeneous fluids. The treatment is generalized to the extent that the waves are supposed to arise on a horizontal stream of incompressible fluid whose density and velocity are arbitrary functions of height, and the upper surface of the fluid is allowed either to be free or to be fixed in a horizontal plane. Explicit formulae for the wave properties and a general interpretation of the physical conditions for the occurrence of the waves are achieved without need to specify particular physical models; but in a later part of the paper, §4, the results are applied to three examples that have been worked out by other means and so provide checks on the present theory. These general results are also shown to accord nicely with the principle of ‘conjugate-flow pairs’ which was explained by Benjamin (1962<jats:italic>b</jats:italic>) with reference to swirling flows along cylindrical ducts, but which is known to apply equally well to flow systems of the kind in question here.</jats:p><jats:p>The theory reveals certain physical peculiarities of a type of flow model often used in theoretical studies of internal-wave phenomena, being specified so as to make the equation for the stream-function linear. In an appendix, some observations are also made regarding the ‘Boussinesq approximation’, which too is often used as a simplifying assumption in this field. It is shown, adding to a recent discussion by Long (1965), that finite internal waves may depend crucially on small effects neglected in this approximation.</jats:p>

title: Entrainment and the structure of turbulent flow
abstract: <jats:p>Although the entrainment of non-turbulent fluid into a turbulent flow occurs across sharply defined boundaries, its rate is not determined solely by the turbulent motion adjacent to the interface but depends on overall properties of the flow, in particular, on those that control the energy balance. In the first place, attention is directed to the many observations which show that the motion in many turbulent shear flows has a structure closely resembling that produced by a rapid, finite, plane shearing of initially isotropic turbulence. The basic reasons for the similarity are the stability and permanence of turbulent eddies and the finite distortions undergone by fluid parcels in free turbulent flows. Next, the existence of eddy similarity and the condition of overall balance of energy are used to account for the variation of entrainment rates within groups of broadly similar flows, in particular mixing layers between streams of different velocities and wall jets on curved surfaces. For some flows which satisfy the ordinary conditions for self-preserving development, no entrainment rate is consistent with the energy balance and self-preserving development is not possible. Examples are the axisymmetric, small-deficit wake and the distorted wake. Finally, the implications of an entrainment rate controlled by the general motion are discussed. It is concluded that the relatively rapid entrainment in a plane wake depends on an active instability of the interface, not present in a constant-pressure boundary layer whose slow rate of entrainment is from ‘passive’ distortion of the bounding surface by eddies of the main turbulent motion. Available observations tend to support this conclusion.</jats:p>

title: Expansions at small Reynolds numbers for the flow past a sphere and a circular cylinder
abstract:
<jats:p>This paper is concerned with the problem of obtaining higher approximations to the flow past a sphere and a circular cylinder than those represented by the well-known solutions of Stokes and Oseen. Since the perturbation theory arising from the consideration of small non-zero Reynolds numbers is a singular one, the problem is largely that of devising suitable techniques for taking this singularity into account when expanding the solution for small Reynolds numbers.</jats:p><jats:p>The technique adopted is as follows. Separate, locally valid (in general), expansions of the stream function are developed for the regions close to, and far from, the obstacle. Reasons are presented for believing that these ‘Stokes’ and ‘Oseen’ expansions are, respectively, of the forms
<jats:inline-formula id="ffm1">$\Sigma \;f_n(R) \psi_n(r, \theta)$</jats:inline-formula> and <jats:inline-formula id="ffm2">$\Sigma \; F_n(R) \Psi_n(R_r, \theta)$</jats:inline-formula>
where (<jats:italic>r</jats:italic>, θ) are spherical or cylindrical polar coordinates made dimensionless with the radius of the obstacle, <jats:italic>R</jats:italic> is the Reynolds number, and <jats:inline-formula id="ffm3">$f_{(n+1)}|f_n$</jats:inline-formula> and <jats:inline-formula id="ffm4">$F_{n+1}|F_n$</jats:inline-formula> vanish with <jats:italic>R.</jats:italic> Substitution of these expansions in the Navier-Stokes equation then yields a set of differential equations for the coefficients ψ<jats:sub><jats:italic>n</jats:italic></jats:sub> and Ψ<jats:sub><jats:italic>n</jats:italic></jats:sub>, but only one set of physical boundary conditions is applicable to each expansion (the no-slip conditions for the Stokes expansion, and the uniform-stream condition for the Oseen expansion) so that unique solutions cannot be derived immediately. However, the fact that the two expansions are (in principle) both derived from the same exact solution leads to a ‘matching’ procedure which yields further boundary conditions for each expansion. It is thus possible to determine alternately successive terms in each expansion.</jats:p><jats:p>The leading terms of the expansions are shown to be closely related to the original solutions of Stokes and Oseen, and detailed results for some further terms are obtained.</jats:p>

title: The development of turbulent boundary layers with negligible wall stress
abstract: <jats:p>In a recent paper, Stratford has described a turbulent boundary layer with continuously zero wall stress and has developed a theory to describe the flow based on two assumptions. The first is that the flow in the outer part of the layer is affected only by the original Reynolds stresses during the initial development, and the second is that flow in the equilibrium layer close to the wall is determined by the pressure gradient and is independent of upstream conditions. In this paper the same assumptions are used, but more careful consideration of their limitations has led to the elimination of some inconsistencies in the original work and to a theory that gives a better description of some of the experimental results. The principal results are: (i) a criterion for zero wall stress in an adverse pressure gradient of sufficient strength, (ii) the form of the pressure distribution for a self-preserving flow with zero stress, (iii) the mean velocity distribution in this flow, (iv) an estimate of the constant in the 'square-root’ velocity distribution for flow near a wall with zero stress.</jats:p>

title: On waves generated in dispersive systems by travelling forcing effects, with applications to the dynamics of rotating fluids
abstract: <jats:p>A theory of the generation of dispersive waves by travelling forcing effects, that may be steady, oscillatory or transient in character, is given for a general homogeneous system. Small disturbances to the system are supposed stable, and governed by a linear partial differential equation with constant coefficients which admits solutions in the form of plane waves satisfying an, in general, anisotropic dispersion relation<jats:italic>P</jats:italic>(σ,<jats:bold>k</jats:bold>) = 0 between frequency σ and wave-number vector<jats:bold>k</jats:bold>.</jats:p><jats:p>If the forcing region, supposed of limited extent, travels with constant velocity<jats:bold>U</jats:bold>, then oscillatory forcing terms of frequency σ<jats:sub>0</jats:sub>(which would be replaced by 0 in the limiting case of a steady forcing effect, while taking, for a typical transient one of duration<jats:italic>T</jats:italic>, values from 0 to about 10/<jats:italic>T</jats:italic>) produce waves of frequency σ<jats:sub>0</jats:sub>+<jats:bold>U. k</jats:bold>(the Doppler effect). For any such waves, the wave-number<jats:bold>k</jats:bold>satisfies the equation<jats:italic>P</jats:italic>(σ<jats:sub>0</jats:sub>+<jats:bold>U.k,k</jats:bold>) = 0, representing a surface in wave-number space here called<jats:italic>S</jats:italic>(σ<jats:sub>0</jats:sub>), and their position relative to that of the forcing region is determined by having been generated when that region was in an earlier position, and having subsequently progressed with the group velocity. This implies the rule, also derived analytically in §§2 and 3, that waves with a particular value of<jats:bold>k</jats:bold>on<jats:italic>S</jats:italic>(σ<jats:sub>0</jats:sub>) are found in a direction, stretching out from the forcing region, which is one of the directions normal to<jats:italic>S</jats:italic>(σ<jats:sub>0</jats:sub>) at<jats:bold>k</jats:bold>, namely the one pointing towards<jats:italic>S</jats:italic>(σ<jats:sub>0</jats:sub>+ δ). This rule is supplemented by results on wave amplitudes and shapes of crests.</jats:p><jats:p>The theory is applied (§§4 and 5) to Rossby waves excited in a beta-plane ocean by travelling patterns of wind stress. If a steady wind-stress pattern moves westward, semicircular waves of length 2π √(<jats:italic>U/β</jats:italic>) trail behind it, but signals are found also directly ahead, consisting of the disturbance integrated in the west-east direction and subjected to a ‘low-pass filter’ with respect to its north-south components of wave-number. An eastward-travelling pattern, by contrast, produces only a wake-like disturbance, calculated in detail in §4. The waves generated for intermediate directions of travel are identified, and the strong tendencies in all cases for westward intensification of transient currents are noted.</jats:p><jats:p>For example, a wind-stress pattern travelling 30° N. of E. leaves a trailing wedge of currents from W. to 30° S. of W. in the steady case. The influence on this conclusion of a finite duration<jats:italic>T</jats:italic>of such a pattern is investigated in §5 by Fourier analysis in time. The fate of Fourier components of frequency σ<jats:sub>0</jats:sub>depends on the ratio L =σ<jats:sub>0</jats:sub>/√(Uβ). If this is less than 1 for all (T, up to about 10/T, then the disturbance retains its trailing character; on the other hand, any components with L > 1 have a much greater directional spread. Tidal terms make fairly small changes to the results, except that (in an ocean of depth H) they make the directional spread disappear for L greater than about √(β<jats:italic>gH</jats:italic>/4f<jats:sup>2</jats:sup>U).</jats:p><jats:p>Excitation of gravity waves in non-rotating fluid is briefly considered, includ-ing generation on deep water by a travelling oscillating disturbance (§6), and generation in a uniformly stratified fluid by a vertically moving obstacle (§7). The predicted wave shapes in the latter case, with cusps at a finite distance behind the obstacle, agree excellently (figures 7 and 8) with experiments by Mowbray (1966).</jats:p><jats:p>An exceptional case, in that part of<jats:italic>S</jats:italic>(σ<jats:sub>0</jats:sub>) is doubly covered, is generation by steady (σ<jats:sub>0</jats:sub>= 0) motion of an obstacle along the axis of uniformly rotating homo-geneous fluid, the surface<jats:italic>S</jats:italic>(0) being a sphere and two coincident planes. Whereas waves corresponding to points on the sphere trail behind the obstacle, the appro-priate normals on the two planes point in opposite directions inside the sphere (§8), permitting the well-known formation of the ‘Taylor column’ ahead of the obstacle at low Rossby numbers.</jats:p><jats:p>Still more complicated, because fully three-dimensional, is the case when the obstacle moves at right angles to the axis of rotation (§9). At finite though small Rossby number it is impossible for the Taylor column formed near the body to extend to large distances from it, where on the contrary the disturbance is shown to take the form of slightly trailing cones, shown in cross-section in figure 12, containing waves whose crests have cusps on the boundaries of the cones. An estimate of the length of the Taylor column, as body dimension divided by Rossby number (for small enough kinematic viscosity), is made by considering the fit between the Taylor-column and wave-cone regions.</jats:p>

title: On the equations of motion for mixtures of liquid and gas bubbles
abstract: <jats:p>On the basis of previous work by the author, equations are derived describing one-dimensional unsteady flow in bubble-fluid mixtures. Attention is subsequently focused on pressure waves of small and moderate amplitude propagating through the mixture. Four characteristic lengths occur, namely, wavelength, amplitude, bubble diameter and inter-bubble distance. The significance of their relative magnitudes for the theory is discussed. It appears that for high gas content the dispersion is weak and then the conservation of mass and momentum lead to equations similar to the Boussinesq equations, describing long dispersive waves of finite amplitude on a fluid of finite depth. For waves propagating in one direction only, the corresponding equation is similar to the Korteweg–de Vries equation.</jats:p><jats:p>It is shown that for mixtures of low gas content the frequency dispersion is in most cases not small. Finally, solutions of the Korteweg–de Vries equation representing cnoidal and solitary waves in a bubble–liquid mixture are given explicitly.</jats:p>

title: Non-linear gravity wave interactions
abstract: <jats:p>In earlier papers Phillips (1960) and Longuet-Higgins (1962) have investigated phase velocity effects and possible resonances associated with the interactions of gravity waves. In this note the problem is discussed from a different viewpoint which demonstrates more clearly the energy-sharing mechanism involved. Equations governing the time dependence of the resonant modes are obtained, rather than the initial growth rate as has been found previously.</jats:p>

title: A computational method for viscous flow problems
abstract: <jats:p>A digital computer method for solving certain problems involving two-dimensional incompressible viscous flow is described. The time-dependent case is treated; the mathematical problem is thus that of solving a non-linear fourth-order partial differential equation in three variables. The choice of difference equations, of relaxation procedure, the kind of approximation to boundary conditions, and the resulting computational stability, speed, and accuracy are considered. Most experience so far has been for a rectangular region for which boundary velocities are prescribed as certain functions of time; an example of one such problem showing vortex formation and break-up is given.</jats:p>

title: The self-propulsion of a deformable body in a perfect fluid
abstract: <jats:p>It is shown that a deformable body can move persistently from rest through a perfect fluid without having to produce vorticity in the fluid.</jats:p>

title: The effect of viscosity and heat conduction on internal gravity waves at a critical level
abstract: <jats:p>The differential equation for the vertical velocity of a gravity wave in an inviscid shear flow is singular at a level where the mean fluid velocity is equal to the horizontal phase velocity of the waves. It has been shown that a wave travelling through such a layer has its amplitude attenuated by a constant factor dependent on the local Richardson number. In this paper the results obtained by solving numerically the full sixth order differential equation, which is derived by including viscosity and heat conduction in the problem, (and is not singular) are discussed, and <jats:italic>the same</jats:italic> attenuation factor is found. Some experiments which confirm certain aspects of the theory are described in an appendix.</jats:p>

title: Finite amplitude celluar convection
abstract: <jats:p>When a layer of fluid is heated uniformly from below and cooled from above, a cellular regime of steady convection is set up at values of the Rayleigh number exceeding a critical value. A method is presented here to determine the form and amplitude of this convection. The non-linear equations describing the fields of motion and temperature are expanded in a sequence of inhomogeneous linear equations dependent upon the solutions of the linear stability problem. We find that there are an infinite number of steady-state finite amplitude solutions (having different horizontal plan-forms) which formally satisfy these equations. A criterion for ‘relative stability’ is deduced which selects as the realized solution that one which has the maximum mean-square temperature gradient. Particular conclusions are that for a large Prandtl number the amplitude of the convection is determined primarily by the distortion of the distribution of mean temperature and only secondarily by the self-distortion of the disturbance, and that when the Prandtl number is less than unity self-distortion plays the dominant role in amplitude determination. The initial heat transport due to convection depends linearly on the Rayleigh number; the heat transport at higher Rayleigh numbers departs only slightly from this linear dependence. Square horizontal plan-forms are preferred to hexagonal plan-forms in ordinary fluids with symmetric boundary conditions. The proposed finite amplitude method is applicable to any model of shear flow or convection with a soluble stability problem.</jats:p>

title: Large-amplitude Benard convection
abstract: <jats:p>Calculations are presented for two-dimensional Bénard convection between free bounding surfaces for ranges of Rayleigh and Prandtl numbers. The variables are expanded in a series consisting of the eigenfunctions of the stability problem and the system is truncated to take into account only a limited number of terms. The amplitudes of the eigenfunctions are evaluated by numerical integration of the resulting non-linear equations. In all cases considered, the system achieves a steady state with the motion consisting of a single large cell. Results for Nusselt number <jats:italic>vs</jats:italic>. Rayleigh number are given for a range of Prandtl number varying between 0·01 and 100 and show that heat flux increases slightly with decreasing Prandtl number. The calculations agree with those of Kuo where the ranges of Rayleigh number overlap. A simple heuristic argument based on the assumption that turbulent boundary layers exist is also given and the conclusions of the latter indicate that heat flux should decrease with decreasing Prandtl number. Thus the behaviour is qualitatively different from that of the calculations. The reason appears to be associated with the fact that the single large cell in the computed cases enables the fluid to accelerate through repeated cycles until it achieves a steady state with the amplitude of the motion much larger than could be acquired by a single turbulent blob free-falling in the gravitational field.</jats:p>

title: Excitation of internal waves in a stably-stratified atmosphere with considerable wind-shear
abstract: <jats:p>The rate of generation of internal waves by a thin turbulent boundary layer was calculated in a previous paper for a stably-stratified atmosphere with no significant wind-shear outside the boundary layer by considering the excitation of normal modes of wave propagation. By using the concept of wave-packets propagating upwards from the boundary layer, the effects of wind-shear can be included. Conditions for the validity of the approximation are given. In general, the spectral distribution of wave-energy at a particular height takes large values in two bands of horizontal wave-number, one band deriving from wave-packets undergoing internal reflexion near that height and the other from wave-packets of very small local frequency that accumulate there. The ‘reflexion’ wave-numbers are dominant if the wind increases with height and the ‘accumulation’ wave-numbers if the wind initially decreases with height. The spectral energy distributions and intensities of the wave-motion are discussed in more detail for an atmosphere of uniform stability and unidirectional wind-shear. The accumulation process may lead to instability or overturning of the waves, and estimates are made of the probable scale and intensity of the ‘clear-air’ turbulence produced. An interesting point is that the rate of energy loss from the boundary layer by radiation of internal waves turns out to be comparable with the rate of production in the outer nine-tenths of the layer, both for atmospheric boundary layers and for the surface layer of the ocean. It seems likely that radiation limits the layer thickness to some extent.</jats:p>

title: Unity and disunity and other mathematical essays
abstract: The book is a collection of 13 essays introduced by a short Preface. Some of them have been already published, some are unpublished. They originated as classroom presentations, formal lectures, sections of author's earlier books, book reviews or things written for own pleasure. Essays are devoted to various subjects such as the unity and disunity of mathematics, evidence in mathematics, visual geometry, mathematical ontology, media vs. mathematics, applied mathematics, mathematics and politics and law, comments on mathematics of literary men. The contents of the book can be described -- as it is done in the Preface -- as a potpourri. It can be an interesting reading matter for all interested in mathematics, both professional mathematicians as well as general public. Also philosophers of mathematics will find it interesting. The author is known for his work in numerical analysis and approximation theory as well as for his investigations in the history and philosophy of mathematics.
venue: Providence, RI: American Mathematical Society (AMS) (ISBN 978-1-4704-2023-9/pbk). ix, 149~p. (2015).

title: Mathematics, art and civilization. Translated from the American by Jochem Berlemann and Marietta Ehret
abstract:
The author (born 1937) is one of the leading figures in contemporary mathematics, who made fundamental contributions to various areas ranging from number theory and algebraic geometry to mathematical physics and algebraic analysis. Simultaneously, he is known as an intellectual and scientist with a tremendous breadth of interests, knowledge, and creativity, whose reflections on the interplay between philosophy, mathematics, physics, logic, language, art, and human civilization are utmost popular and appreciated. In this context, his book [Mathematics as metaphor. Selected essays of Yuri I. Manin. Providence, RI: American Mathematical Society (AMS) (2007; Zbl 1172.00003)] is one of the most prominent examples, which is now followed by the German translation of a further essay titled ``Mathematics, art, civilisation'' [unpublished, (2009)]. The theme of this essay is the development of natural and formal languages with regard to mathematical concepts, numbers, abstract structures, and geometric configurations.

 More precisely, the author depicts the connection between mathematical thinking and expression, on the one hand, and the development of art and civilization on the other, thereby analyzing the persuasive power of mathematics within the latter through concrete examples and instructive illustrations. Among the topics touched upon are: mathematics and the history of mankind, the development of number systems, formal languages, geometry, diagrams and categories, the foundations of mathematics, the relation between discreteness and continuity, the role of mathematics in our civilization, and the linking ideas between them, art, and general human civilization. As it is pointed out in the preface to this enlightening booklet, the target audience are readers interested in formal languages, history of art, and with a sense for abstraction. For those readers, the author once more offers a wealth of unexpected insights into the multifarious relations between mathematics and society, without assuming any higher mathematical education on their part.
venue: Die Weltweit Besten Mathematischen Artikel im 21. Jahrhundert 3. Lemgo: e-enterprise (ISBN 978-3-945059-15-9/pbk; 978-3-945059-16-6/ebook). 112~p. (2014).

title: The protean character of mathematics
abstract:
The author discusses some ideas from his seminal book ``Mathematics: form and function'' [Springer, Berlin/New York (1986; Zbl 0660.00019 and Zbl 0675.00001)]. He particularly discusses the thesis that mathematics is protean: ``This means that one and the same mathematical structure has many different empirical realizations. Thus, mathematics provides common overarching forms, each of which can and does serve to describe different aspects of the external world. This places mathematics in relation to the other parts of science: mathematics is that part of science which applies in more than one empirical context'' (p. 1). 


He gives some evidence for this thesis by presenting examples from arithmetic, geometry, and analysis. He hints at unexpected applications and mathematical forms in different contexts, e.g., tensor product spaces, the concept of a group, the Riemann-Roch formula, relations between abstract algebraical concepts and the conformal field theory in theoretical physics, and finally the \(\lambda\)-calculus. 


According to the author the intrinsically formal character of protean mathematics gives the reason that mathematics is essentially axiomatic. It follows furthermore that mathematics does not need foundations: ``Any proposed foundation purports to say that mathematics is about this or that fundamental thing. But mathematics is not about things but about form. In particular, mathematics is not about sets'' (p. 9). The author admits that there are a lot of dead ends in mathematics which can be realized with the help of a ``good understanding of the nature of mathematics''. He lists fuzzy topology spaces, the notions of a groupoid and non-toral graphs, the factorization of large numbers. 


For the author mathematics is a ``tightly connected network of different forms and concepts'' (p. 11) in which progress becomes manifest in two complementary tasks: the solution of outstanding problems and the understanding of achieved results. As an example for the long process of mathematical understanding he gives the main steps in the development of Galois theory between the work of Lagrange and 1990. 


The author concludes with the wish ``May protean understanding prosper!'' His conception gives a home for all the recent studies on the rôle of analogy in the development of mathematics. It proposes a philosophy of mathematics based on the still rather vague notion of ``the formal''. Although the author gets rid of platonistic, realistic, naturalistic, etc. mathematical objects, he does not prove that mathematics needs no foundations at all, he himself presents one.

For the entire collection see [Zbl 0839.00019].
venue: Echeverria, Javier (ed.) et al., The space of mathematics. Philosophical, epistemological, and historical explorations. Revised papers from a symposium on structures in mathematical theories, Donostia/San Sebastian, Basque Country, Spain, September 1990. Berlin: Walter de Gruyter. Grundlagen der Kommunikation und Kognition. 1-13 (1992).

title: Was erforschen die Mathematiker?
abstract: Il s'agit d'une conférence s'adressant à un auditoire de spécialistes de toutes les disciplines scientifiques. L'A. commence par un rapide survol de l'histoire des mathématiques; en partant des calculs des Babyloniens, il décrit l'apport essentiel des Grecs, étroitement lié à leur philosophie, les découvertes du \(17^ e\) siècle en algèbre, géométrie projective analyse et Calcul des probabilités, et leurs remarquables applications à l'astronomie et la physique. Il insiste sur la manière dont les notions mathématiques se sont précisées et mieux organisées, d'abord par l'introduction par Cantor de la théorie des ensembles, et la dissociation des diverses sortes de structures; puis, à la suite des ''paradoxes'', la formalisation du langage; sous l'influence d'Emmy Noether, la classification des mathématiques suivant les structures qui y interviennent. Il montre que le développement des mathématiques n'a pas été arbitraire, mais un effort conscient vers la simplicité logique et la clarté. Comme tous les mathématiciens, il regrette que leur science demande tant d'abstraction et de constructions mentals, qui en rendent l'abord difficile pour les mathématiciens, alors qu'elles ont rendu beaucoup plus faciles les communications entre les diverses branches des mathématiques. Il termine en énumérant quelques-uns des progrès les plus récents (groupes d'homotopie, théorie des schémas et ses applications, notamment à la conjecture de Mordell, géométrie différentielle et conjecture de Calabi, théorème de Riemann-Roch); cette dernière partie n'est évidemment accessible qu'aux mathématiciens.
venue: Abh. Math.-Naturwiss. Kl., Akad. Wiss. Lit. 1986, Nr. 3, 17 S. (1986).

title: Just what is constructive mathematics? Introduction. With a preface by N. M. Nagornyĭ.
abstract:
[For the entire collection see Zbl 0641.00007.] 


N. M. Nagornyi explains in the introduction to A. A. Markov's `What is constructive mathematics' how Markov starting with the study of chemistry got interested in theoretical physics, then mathematics and then finally in the foundations of mathematics, particularly constructive mathematics. According to Markov both the opposing views that mathematics cannot be considered as a science, since it does not deal with experiments/experience and that mathematics is a natural science (Engels) since it deals with forms of time and space surrounding us are inadequate. Markov affirms that mathematics deals with experience since we count objects in space and time. In as much as it however constructs digits and formulas which are applied in other sciences to gain useful knowledge it should be considered as a part of technology. One might contrast this with Brouwer's philosophy of constructive mathematics where mathematical objects are considered as free creations of the human mind which have nothing to do with technology.
venue: Rules in the development of modern mathematics, Methodol. Aspects, Moskva 1987, 209-212 (1987).

title: Is God a mathematician? Why the book of nature is written in the language of mathematics. Translated from the English by Susanne Kuhlmann-Krieg
abstract:
Erst Anfang 2021, also elf Jahre nach Erscheinen der ersten Auflage der deutschen Fassung von Mario Livios \textit{Ist Gott ein Mathematiker}, bin ich von zbMATH für eine Rezension des Buches angefragt worden. Mir selbst liegt die 7.~Auf{\null}lage des Buchs aus dem Jahr 2020 vor. Da ich der Meinung bin, dass auch verspätete Rezensionen oder Rezensionen späterer Auf{\null}lagen sinnvoll sein können, habe ich die Rezensionsanfrage gern angenommen. Die verspätete Anfertigung dieser Buchbesprechung hat aber auch gewisse Vorteile, denn bei der Anfrage stellte ich sofort fest, dass das Buch bereits intensiv in den Print- und Onlinemedien der deutschen Presse besprochen wurde. Die Stimmen waren größtenteils positiv, und auch insofern habe ich die Lektüre gern auf mich genommen.

Bestimmt könnte man in den Kanon des deutschen Feuilletons miteinsteigen und Livios Werk als gewinnbringende Lektüre auf dem Gebiet des allgemeinverständlichen Sachbuchs kennzeichnen: die Übersetzung scheint einwandfrei zu sein; der unterhaltsame und meist kurzweilige Text bietet einen Streifzug und Rundumblick über Themen aus der Gebiet der populären Philosophie der Mathematik mit einigen Ausflügen in Bereiche wie Physik, Genetik, Kognitionswissenschaft, Astrophysik, etc.; das Buch ist durchgängig und meist sinnvoll bebildert; weder mathematische noch philosophische Vorkenntnisse werden vorausgesetzt; die Vorgehensweise ist systematisch größtenteils nach mathematischen Fachbereichen und Themen unterteilt (Kap.~2: Numerologie und Platonismus; Kap.~3: Mechanik und Astrophysik; Kap.~4: analytische Geometrie und Mechanik; Kap.~5: Statistik und Wahrscheinlichkeit; Kap.~6: Geometrie; Kap.~7: Logik); historisch folgt der Text der Ideengeschichte von der Antike bis zur Moderne mit einigen Schwerpunkten (bspw. Kap.~2: Pythagoras, Platon; Kap.~3: Archimedes, Galilei; Kap.~4: Descartes, Newton u.a.). Rund herum ein gut verständliches Sachbuch, das Laien die Welt der Mathematik und Philosophie näherbringen kann.

Da ich aber keine Rezension für den Kulturteil in der deutschen Presse, sondern für ein wissenschaftliches Rezensionsorgan verfassen soll, möchte ich in dieser Rezension noch einen anderen Maßstab an den Text anlegen: Folgt das Buch den heute herrschenden Meinungen in der Philosophie der Mathematik? Oder anders gefragt: Kann der Text auch für professionelle Philosophen oder Mathematiker gewinnbringend sein? Die Antwort auf diese Fragen muss differenziert erfolgen. Zunächst kann ich behaupten, dass ich durch einige Punkte bei der Lektüre zum weiteren Nachdenken angeregt wurde. Zudem habe ich mehrere Zitate berühmter Wissenschaftler gefunden (Wissenschaftlerinnen werden leider kaum berücksichtigt), die ich noch nicht kannte oder zumindest nicht im Kontext des behandelten Themas als gedeihlich in Erinnerung hatte.

Anders sieht es aber aus, wenn man sowohl auf die Details schaut als auch die Hauptthese überdenkt. Die Hauptthese des Werks besagt, dass das Buch der Natur in der Sprache der Mathematik geschrieben sei. Die Argumente für diese These sind in meinen Augen nicht ausreichend begründet oder vielleicht einfach nicht ernst gemeint. Zum Ende des Buchs verliert der Autor auch diese Hauptthese aus dem Blick. Bei den Detailfragen, bei denen ich der Meinung bin, mich auch in der Forschung intensiver mit den Themen beschäftigt zu haben, zeigen sich aber Mängel, was die Einbeziehung und die Darstellung des aktuellen Forschungsstandes betrifft. (Das gilt auch für das Jahr 2009, in dem die erste Auf{\null}lage des Werks erfolgte.) Ich greife im Folgenden zwei Beispiele für die Detailprobleme heraus, nämlich Platonismus (Kap.~1) und Logik (Kap.~7), und komme zuletzt auf die Hauptthese zu sprechen.

Aus philosophiehistorischer und philologischer Sicht halte ich die Zuschreibung der hier behandelten Argumente an Platon als Autor für höchst problematisch. Die Grundfrage, die sich dem Philosophen in Kap.~1 stellt, lautet: Hat Platon tatsächlich all diese Themen vertreten, die Livio anführt? Die meisten Platonforscher der letzten 50 Jahre würden sagen: Nein, denn es waren unterschiedliche Dialogfiguren und nicht Platon selbst, die die hier aufgeführten Positionen vertreten. Insbesondere seit den 1980er Jahren hat die Platonforschung eine überaus differenzierte Sprechweise entwickelt, um den Inhalt platonischer Dialoge zu untersuchen und darzustellen. Einen guten Eindruck von der Komplexität der Platonforschung erhält man beispielsweise durch G.~Press \textit{Who Speaks for Plato?} aus dem Jahr 2000. Das hier vorliegende Kapitel ``In Platons Höhle'' zeigt aber keine Kenntnis von der Komplexität, mit der man platonische Texte interpretieren muss. Livio selbst interpretiert auch nicht, sondern er schafft mit einigen (manchmal sogar revisionsbedürftigen) Fakten nur Übergänge zwischen längeren Zitaten, die aber weder erklärt noch kontextualisiert werden. Ziel des Kapitels scheint es allein zu sein, kurz zu skizzieren, was man in der Philosophie der Mathematik als `Platonismus' bezeichnet.

Weitere Detailprobleme lassen sich auch im Kapitel ``Logiker: Nachdenken über das Denken'' benennen. Hier wird dem Leser eine derart verkürzte Logikgeschichte geboten, dass man sich quasi an die Kampfschriften des frühen 20.~Jahrhunderts erinnert fühlt. Tatsächlich sind es auch viele der Protagonisten der damaligen Zeit, die hier als Helden herhalten müssen: De Morgan, Boole, Frege, Russell, Whitehead, Zermelo, Gödel, Hilbert. Dass die gegenwärtige Logik weitaus facettenreicher ist als die damaligen Programmschriften, bleibt unberücksichtigt. Einzelne Aussagen entsprechen zudem nicht dem Stand der Forschung: Aussagenlogische Schlüsse waren auch vor dem 20. Jahrhundert bekannt; Leibniz war nicht der erste, der für eine Ähnlichkeit zwischen Mathematik und Logik argumentiert hat; Mitte des 19.~Jahrhunderts kam auch die Logik nicht ``wieder mehr in Mode'', das war sie bereits; quantifizierte Prädikate gab es auch gelegentlich vor De Morgan u.v.a.

Mir ist bewusst, dass man nicht darum herumkommt, Allgemeinaussagen zu machen, wenn man ein verständliches Sachbuch schreibt, in dem man auf etwa 300 Seiten die nach eigenem Dafürhalten größten Ideen von der Antike bis zur Gegenwart skizzieren möchte. Dennoch wäre eine intensivere Miteinbeziehung der gegenwärtigen Forschung gewinnbringend gewesen: An vielen Stellen bleibt die Detailarbeit auf dem Stand der Philosophie- und Mathematikgeschichte des frühen 20. Jahrhunderts stehen. Was schließlich auf dem heutigen Markt der Populärliteratur fehlt, ist nicht allgemeinverständliche Darstellung der Auffassungen des frühen 20. Jahrhunderts, sondern eine komprimierte Darstellung, was die heutige Forschung zu den jeweiligen Themen sagt. So lange eine derartige Schrift fehlt, kann man auch auf klassische Werke zurückgreifen, die eine profundere Philosophie der Mathematik liefern, z.B. Oskar Beckers \textit{Grundlagen der Mathematik in geschichtlicher Entwicklung}.

Nun kann man aber meinen, dass viele der Detailfragen sowieso nur auf die Beantwortung der Leitfrage des Buches abzielen. Das Leitthema des Buches dreht sich um die Frage, ob das Buch der Natur in der Sprache der Mathematik geschrieben sei. Um diese vom Autor bejahte Frage drehen sich zwei weitere: Ist Gott ein Mathematiker und werden mathematische Erkenntnisse erfunden oder entdeckt? Alle drei Fragen hängen eng zusammen, denn schließlich kann man argumentieren, dass, wenn es ein Buch der Natur gibt, es auch einen Autor desselben geben muss. Und hat ein Autor ein Buch der Natur geschrieben, so werden wir bei der Entschlüsselung des Buchs der Natur natürlich Zahlen entdecken und müssen diese nicht selbst erfinden.

Das Buch hat somit einen philosophischen Kern und Livios Methode besteht vor allem darin, Autoritäten anzuführen, die für einen Platonismus und gegen einen Konstruktivismus sprechen. Diese Helden des Buchs scheinen Livios These zu stützen, dass das Buch der Natur in der Sprache der Mathematik geschrieben sei. Mit Kapitel~8 (``Unbegreifliche Erklärungsmacht?'') scheint also die Leitfrage mit `Ja' beantwortet zu sein und es geht, wie am Ende des Kapitels angekündigt, nur noch um die Frage, warum das Buch der Natur in der Sprache der Mathematik geschrieben sei und ob die Mathematik entdeckt oder erfunden wird. Hinsichtlich der letzten Frage scheint Livio zu schwanken: Einerseits beantwortet er die Frage mit einem ``Sowohl \dots\ als auch'', andererseits gibt er an mehreren Stellen zu verstehen, dass er eher zur Entdeckungshypothese neigt.

Aus philosophiehistorischer und philosophischer Sicht kann man auch gegen Livios Leitthese und der Entdeckungs-Erfindungshypothese mehrere Kritikpunkte anbringen. Wie so häufig bei populären Sachbüchern aus dem englischsprachigen Raum sind zwar die Fragen philosophischer Natur, aber nicht die Methode: Livio greift nicht auf differenzierte Definitionen und Argumente aus der Forschungsliteratur der Philosophie der Mathematik zurück, sondern er sammelt Bonmots und biographische Notizen von Mathematikern und Naturwissenschaftlern, denen er aufgrund ihrer Erfolge in ihrem Fachgebiet auch eine philosophische Kompetenz zuschreibt. Untermauert wird die Autorität meist durch Hinweise, wie etwa dass ein Urheber eines bestimmten Zitats bspw. ``Nobelpreisträger'' oder einfach ``der größte Mathematiker des 20.~Jahrhunderts'' ist.

Das Ergebnis dieser auf dem argumentum ad verecundiam basierenden Methode ist eine dialektische Phänomenologie, die den Kompilator vor die Herausforderung stellt, Sinn und Einheit oder zumindest Textbrücken zwischen den heterogenen Zitaten zu schaffen, die ja aufgrund der Kompetenzen ihrer Urheber irgendwie alle Recht zu haben scheinen. Der Kompilator unterscheidet letztlich gar nicht mehr zwischen dem Stellenwert der Argumente: So wird beispielsweise bei der `Auswertung' der Zitate nicht mehr unterschieden, ob nun die Genese oder die Geltung für eine bestimmte im Zitat ausgedrückte Position entscheidend ist. Aus philosophischer Sicht wirkt das Ende von Livios Buch somit wie ein Sammelsurium von undifferenzierten Argumenten, die in einer scheinbaren Beliebigkeit in Beziehung gesetzt werden. Die Frage, ob nun Mathematik auf Erfindung oder Entdeckung beruht, wird sich nicht durch eine Hitliste von Zitaten berühmter Naturwissenschaftler lösen lassen.

Auch gegen die das Buch der Natur betreffenden Leitthese, die noch einmal im vorletzten Kapiteln an einigen Textstellen durchschimmert, lassen sich einige Gegenargumente anführen. Ich belasse es hier bei zwei Gegenargumenten, einmal aus der Sicht der Wissenschaftsgeschichte und einmal aus philosophischer Perspektive: Aus der ersten Sicht kann man potentiellen Lesern, die sich für diese Frage interessieren, empfehlen, einen Blick in die Forschung zu werfen, die vor allem durch Hans Blumenbergs Traktat \textit{Die Lesbarkeit der Welt} angeregt wurde. Durch diese Lektüre dürfte bereits deutlich werden, dass Livios Argumentation für ein Buch der Natur in der Sprache der Mathematik insgesamt unzureichend definiert und überaus reduktiv ist. Die Metapher des Buchs der Natur wird nicht hinreichend erklärt und die historischen Positionen, die sich zu dieser Metapher verhalten, werden nur notdürftig voneinander abgegrenzt. Aus zweiter Sicht muss sich dem Leser von popluärwissenschaftlichen Büchern, die diese These vertreten, immer wieder eine Frage aufdrängen: Warum ist die hier vertretene Hauptthese nicht auch in der Sprache der Mathematik geschrieben? Derjenige Gelehrte, der die Wissenschafts- und Philosophiegeschichte nicht als Bestätigung der eigenen Arbeitsweise konsultiert oder sein eigenes Tun beim Schreiben eines Buches reflektiert, weiß, dass Quantität nicht der einzige Kategorientitel ist, mit dem man in der Semantik und Ontologie klassifiziert.

title: On some peculiarities of 20th century mathematics
abstract: Summary: During the XXth century the nature of mathematics changed. First of all the scale of mathematics changed radically, as the number of mathematicians increased a hundredfold during the century. Second, the borders and the subjects of study of mathematics increased markedly. Third, earlier mathematics was developed primarily in a national context; today mathematical research is international in nature. The article examines the sources of mathematical creativity, mathematics and nature, civilian and military applications of mathematics, mathematics and philosophy, and the main mathematical problems of the XXth century.

title: Freedom in mathematics. Translated from the French
abstract:
This book makes the reader a participant of an interesting conversation of two mathematicians, Pierre Cartier, emeritus CNRS research director, research scientist at the Institut des Hautes Etudes Scientifiques of Bures-sur-Yvette, former member of the Bourbaki group, and Cédric Villani, director of the Henri Poincaré Institute, professor at the University of Lyon, Fields medalist in 2010, with Jean Dhombres, mathematician and historian of science, emeritus CNRS research director, director of studies at the Alexandre Koyre Centre of the École des hautes études en sciences sociales and Gerhard Heinzmann, professor of philosophy at the University of Lorraine, director of the Maison des sciences de l'homme of Lorraine.

Leading the discussion, Jean Dhombre and Gerhard Heinzmann confront the points of view of two prominent mathematicians on a wide spectrum of topics centered around three major directions: the relationship between mathematics and reality, the freedom that mathematics offered for the development of natural sciences and mathematics itself, and the responsibility required to address the role of mathematics in research and educational policies. Starting with an insight into the origins of mathematics and its development as a fact of civilization, the authors first turn their attention to the complex interplay between mathematics and reality, the nature of mathematical objects, aesthetic criteria in mathematics, predictability and chaos. The next exciting topic raised in the conversation regards the role of mathematics in education, didactics of mathematics, mathematical culture, the role of applied mathematics in mathematics education and the importance of teaching the history of mathematics. The discussion concludes with the exploration of the nature and challenges of mathematics research. The authors talk about convergence between fundamental and applied mathematics, interplay between mathematics and industry, organization and development of mathematics research, the role of mathematics in the world and the share of French mathematics in the global mathematical heritage.

In the final section, Professor Villani points out that ``the principle of this informal discussion between people from different generations and with different views, going from one topic to the other, is I think well adapted to the multifaceted activity which mathematics is: science, art, social activity; with its own coherence and rules, but also in interaction with other human activities; with a long rich history, but always very modern.'' The authors complement their interesting discourse with reading lists inviting the reader to reflect further on the topics debated in the book and related issues. A very stimulating reading full of interesting thoughts and opinions.

title: Mathematics - science and profession. (Matematika - nauka i professiya). Comp. by G. A. Gal'perin
abstract: This is a collection of popular articles addressed at high school students and their teachers. They are grouped in four sections: (I) Reflexions of a mathematician; (II) Fundamental concepts of school mathematics; (III) Popular lectures for high school students; and (IV) Lectures for teachers. All these papers had been published before the appearance of this collection. Section I contains memoirs of A. N. Kolmogorov (about himself, about other mathematicians), his thoughts about mathematics and mathematical profession, about interconnections between mathematics and ``reality''. Section II consists of the papers dedicated to the principal concepts and methods of ``school'' (that is, pre-college) mathematics: functions, graphs, transformations, approximate calculations, angle measures, vectors, the logic of formula-building, etc. These papers are addressed at secondary school students. Section III is for those high school students who love mathematics and who can become mathematicians. It contains articles about probabilities, graphs in logarithmic scale, Euclid's algorithm, Hilbert's tenth problem, etc. There are a few problems Kolmogorov suggested to high school students, one of them has not been solved. The last section contains Kolmogorov's lectures for high school teachers of mathematics on the fundamenals of school mathematics, on dialectical materialism in the school courses of mathematics and physics, on connections between mathematics and other sciences and technology.
venue: Bibliotechka ``Kvant'', Vyp. 64. Moskva: ``Nauka''. Glavnaya Redaktsiya Fiziko-Matematicheskoj Literatury. 288 p. R. 0.65 (1988).

title: Values and ethics in mathematics
abstract:
The author of the article has published four books, three of them with coauthors. Now he intends to focus on two aspects of everyday lives and activities of mathematicians. He addresses the relations to colleagues and the ethical values of mathematical production, referring to Goethe's autobiographic novel `Dichtung und Wahrheit aus meinem Leben', Dichtung meaning both poetry and invention. 

His views are illustrated by several specific examples.

Speaking about the unmercifully abstraction of the Bourbaki collection, he admits that it may help research. Recalling his reluctance to Dieudonné's presentation of the foundations of algebraic geometry due to Grothendieck, he later changed his appreciation after having heard about Gert Faltings' achievements. He concludes that there is definitely not a unique point of view for all mathematics. Yet of course he condemns Bieberbach's assignment of types of mathematics to certain races, the so called `bad races'.

As for quarrels raised by priority claims, the author describes the scientific and political attacks launched against Lusin, summing up by asserting that Alexandroff was very close to doing what he himself had done during the trial, i.e., the appropriation of Suslin's work.

Opposing Kummer's sad expectation to leave beauty to tailors and shoemakers, the author feels the beauty of a proof if it is deeply understood and performs a surprising twist.

The reading of this article may be recommended to any mathematician willing to see beyond -- and may suggest occasions for further contributions to the topics.

For the entire collection see [Zbl 1024.00029].
venue: Kopotun, Kirill (ed.) et al., Trends in approximation theory. Papers from the internatinal symposium in honor of the 60th birthday of Larry L. Schumaker, Nashville, TX, USA, May 17--20, 2000. Nashville, TX: Vanderbilt University Press (ISBN 0-8265-1379-4/hbk). Innovations in Applied Mathematics, 273-294 (2001).

title: Was ist Mathematik. Übers. aus dem Engl. von Iris Runge. Bearb. der Übers. von Arnold Kirsch und Brigitte Rellich
abstract: Vgl. die Besprechungen früherer Auflagen (Berlin 1962; Zbl 0118.24103) und (Torino 1950; Zbl 0041.37409).

title: Pourquoi je fais des mathématiques
abstract: This article is a personal statement of why the author does mathematics. Initially he discusses the ideal of a search for truth compared with a good career. He then discusses mathematics itself considering the concepts that mathematics is beautiful, interesting and useful. He summarises some of the discussion by the sentence: The doing of interesting mathematics creates a state of excitement! When he discusses the usefulness of mathematics he is more concerned whether the theorems are very general rather than applications to other disciplines. Finally he discusses the place of intuition in mathematics and the fact that following the intuitive leap one needs to justify the steps. This gives rise often to difficulties but without these difficulties mathematics would be too vague or too facile.

title: The mathematical experience. Study edition. With an introduction by Gian-Carlo Rota
abstract: This is a reprint of the 1995 edition [Zbl 0837.00001] of a well-known and popular text. In a new edition, each of the authors added a brief essay in the end. Philip J. Davis listed a number of new applications of mathematics (search engines, bioinformatics, computer vision, computational finance, etc.) that have become prominent since the appearance of \textit{The Mathematical Experience}. In the Philosophical Afterword, Reuben Hersh briefly commented on a few famous instances in the last few decades that are directly related to topics in the book. These are Wile's proof of Fermat's last theorem, Perelman's proof of the Poincaré conjecture, wavelets as a generalization of Fourier analysis, fractals as a new kind of non-Euclidean geometry, and random matrices in connection with the Riemann hypothesis. Finally, in the Pedagogical Afterword, Elena Anne Corie Marchisotto commented on the twenty five years of the use of the study edition both in lower and upper division general education courses at California State University Northridge and by the wider audience. A warmly welcomed reprint of a very nice book that can be recommended for teaching, self-education, and simply as an entertaining reading.
venue: Modern Birkhäuser Classics. Berlin: Springer (ISBN 978-0-8176-8294-1/pbk; 978-0-8176-8295-8/ebook). xxv, 500~p. (2012).

title: Mathematical gems. II
abstract:
This book is a most fitting, and welcome, companion to the author's earlier volume with (almost) the same title (Washington 1973) see Zbl 0281.00004. The fourteen chapters are independent of one another and deal with a charming variety of topics from geometry, number theory, combinatorics and graph theory. While the mathematics involved rarely, if ever, goes beyond the high school level it is safe to say that almost every mathematician will find something new and interesting in almost every essay. 

Among the questions discussed are: How many guards are needed in a art gallery if every picture is to be constantly protected?; If \(n\) points are positioned in the plane how many different distances can occur?; How many times can the minimum (maximum) distance occur?; How many steps will an application of the Euclidean algorithm require?; Is there a simple formula which will generate only primes and each odd prime exactly once?; How can those graphs which permit disjoint \(n\) couplings'' of their vertices be characterized? 

Each of these questions, and many others, is answered carefully, completely and lucidly by the author. The title suggests the possibility that more collections of ''gems'' are being considered by Prof. Honsberger. We would like to encourage him in his efforts and look forward with anticipation to volume three.
venue: The Dolciani Mathematical Expositions. No. 2. Washington, D.C.: The Mathematical Association of America. ix, 182 p. \$10.00 (1976).

title: Where mathematics comes from. How the embodied mind brings mathematics into being
abstract:
This book performed by a linguist and a psychologist is definitely controversial. It deals with the cognitive science of mathematical ideas; it is about `human mathematics', conceptualized by human brains. The thesis claims that human mathematics \textit{is} mathematics.


We quote: ``The only mathematics that human beings know is a mind-based mathematics, linked and structured by human brains and minds.'' Mathematics comes from us! It is a product of the now a bit better kown capacities of the human brain and not a merely contingent social construction. Wigner's famous `effectiveness of mathematics in the natural science' is often explained saying that mathematics itself is encompassed in the very structure of the physical universe. The authors' views are clearly opposite to platonism. Even if many mathematicians agree that mathematics actually constitutes a human activity, they would probably attribute a higher degree of freedom and liberty to it, hence making the mathematician's profession the more fascinating. A logical framework is indispensable, but mathematics does not reduce to a tautology. 


Also the historical development of mathematics does not proceed at random nor by logical constraints; it is due to a lot of `outer' influences, may they be philosophical, social or even military demands, and of course problems raised in other sciences. Yet the conceptual mechanism of the `embodied' human mind is naturally used as it is evolved in the real world. The distribution of prime numbers, the symmetry of objects can they be motivated by the mere configuration of the human mind? If polar coordinates had appeared ahead of Cartesian coordinates, would not aspects of mathematics have shown up differently, at least during a certain time? How to situate the influence of Gödel's theorem, often claimed to assert platonic convictions? What are the reasons for not being able to conceive the `set of all sets'? `The portrait of mathematics has a human face!' Could not the same be claimed for physical theories, quantum theory for instance? Obviously, the physical models that we are constructing also reflect our mental perceptions. 


The authors list systematized enumerations of mathematical procedures in various specific fields. For instance, as characteristic properties are mentioned: Universality, precision, consistency, stability, generalizability, discoverability. The nonplatonist may be disappointed that a certain creativity (obviously not ex nihilo) in mathematics is not sufficiently acknowledged, but may appreciate that mathematics is fundamentally seen as a human activity.


A well written stimulating book.
venue: New York, NY: Basic Books. xvii, 493 p. (2000).

title: Mathletics. How gamblers, managers, and sports enthusiasts use mathematics in baseball, basketball, and football
abstract: The book discusses mathematical formulas and techniques for such things as evaluating players or teams, or strategies, predicting outcomes of games, series of games or full seasons. Part I deals with baseball, Part II with football (i.e., USA football), Part III with basketball and part IV with other topics. Sample topics in Part I: Evaluating Hitters by Linear Weights, Evaluating Baseball Pitchers and Forecasting Future Pitcher Performance, Baseball Decision-Making, The Value of Replacement Players. Part II: Football States and Values, Is Passing Better Than Running:, Should We Go for a One-Point or Two-Point Conversion?, To Give Up the Ball Is Better Than to Receive, How Valuable are High Draft Picks in the NFL? Part III: Basketball Statistics 101--The Four-Factor Model, NBA Lineup Analysis, Are College Basketball Games Fixed? End-Game Basketball Strategy. Part IV: Sports Gambling 101, \textit{Freakonomics} Meets the Bookmaker, The Rating Percentage Index, Can Money Buy Success?, Comparing Players from Different Eras.
venue: Princeton, NJ: Princeton University Press (ISBN 978-0-691-13913-5/hbk). xvii, 358~p. (2009).

title: The history of mathematics: a source-based approach. Volume 1
abstract:
Influenced to some extent by [\textit{J. Fauvel} (ed.) and \textit{J. Gray} (ed.), The history of mathematics: a reader. Reprint of the 1987 edition. Basingstoke: Macmillan. Milton Keynes: The Open University (1996; Zbl 0931.01001)], this is a history of mathematics from early counting and Egyptian mathematics to European mathematics in the early 17th century. While the main part of the book consists of the authors' narrative about the history of mathematics, there are examples of original texts throughout the book, which are, with the exception of the mathematical fragment (Socrates and the slave boy) in Plato's \textit{Menon}, no more than one page long.

The choice of topics is a very appealing one, with a short chapter on Egyptian and Mesopotamian mathematics, followed by four chapters on Greek mathematics (Plato, Aristotle, the development of proof, the classical geometric construction problems, Archimedes and curves, the Hellenistic world, Ptolemy, Diophantus), one chapter on mathematics in India and China, one on the mathematics of the Islamic world, one on the medieval Christian West, one on the solution of cubics and quartics, the Renaissance, Bombelli and Viète, one on the Renaissance of mathematics in Britain, one on the astronomical revolution (Copernicus, Kepler, and Galileo), and one on French mathematics in the early 17th century (Fermat and Descartes).
venue: AMS/MAA Textbooks 45. Providence, RI: MAA Press/American Mathematical Society (AMS) (ISBN 978-1-4704-4352-8/hbk; 978-1-4704-5064-9/ebook; 978-1-4704-6499-8/set). xiii, 488~p. (2019).

title: Why is there philosophy of mathematics at all?
abstract:
The author's main problem is well reflected in the book's title: why is there a kind of perennial need for a philosophy of mathematics, why are there some equally perennial problems, and why is this situation different for mathematics compared with fields like physics or economics?

The book offers some explications of problems and approaches toward them rather than sketches of solutions.
